Consideration of the risks includes not only the downside risk, but also the potential for upside.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B9\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"To have power over an investee, an investor must have **existing rights that give it the current ability to direct the relevant activities**. For the purpose of assessing power, **only substantive rights and rights that are not protective shall be considered** (see paragraphs B22⁠–⁠B28).\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B17\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"When voting rights cannot have a significant effect on an investee’s returns, such as when voting rights relate to administrative tasks only and **contractual arrangements determine the direction of the relevant activities**, the investor needs to assess those contractual arrangements in order to determine whether it has rights sufficient to give it power over the investee. To determine whether an investor has rights sufficient to give it power, the investor shall consider the purpose and design of the investee (see paragraphs B5⁠–⁠B8) and the requirements in paragraphs B51⁠–⁠B54 together with paragraphs B18⁠–⁠B20.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B22\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"An investor, in assessing whether it has power, considers **only substantive rights** relating to an investee (held by the investor and others). For a right to be substantive, the holder must have the practical ability to exercise that right.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B26\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In evaluating whether rights give an investor power over an investee, the investor shall assess whether its rights, and rights held by others, are **protective rights**. Protective rights relate to fundamental changes to the activities of an investee or apply in exceptional circumstances. However, not all rights that apply in exceptional circumstances or are contingent on events are protective (see paragraphs B13 and B53).\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B27\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Because protective rights are designed to protect the interests of their holder **without giving that party power over the investee** to which those rights relate, an investor that holds only protective rights **cannot have power or prevent another party from having power** over an investee (see paragraph 14).\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B51\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In assessing the purpose and design of an investee (see paragraphs B5⁠–⁠B8), an investor shall consider the involvement and decisions made at the investee’s inception as part of its design and evaluate whether the transaction terms and features of the involvement provide the investor with rights that are sufficient to give it power. Being involved in the design of an investee alone is not sufficient to give an investor control. However, involvement in the design may indicate that the investor had the opportunity to obtain rights that are sufficient to give it power over the investee.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B52\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In addition, an investor shall consider contractual arrangements such as call rights, put rights and liquidation rights established at the investee’s inception. When these contractual arrangements involve activities that are closely related to the investee, then these activities are, in substance, an integral part of the investee’s overall activities, even though they may occur outside the legal boundaries of the investee. Therefore, **explicit or implicit decision‑making rights embedded in contractual arrangements** that are closely related to the investee need to be considered as relevant activities when determining power over the investee.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B53\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"For some investees, relevant activities occur only when particular circumstances arise or events occur. The investee may be designed so that the direction of its activities and its returns are predetermined unless and until those particular circumstances arise or events occur. In this case, **only the decisions about the investee’s activities when those circumstances or events occur can significantly affect its returns and thus be relevant activities**. The circumstances or events need not have occurred for an investor with the ability to make those decisions to have power. **The fact that the right to make decisions is contingent on circumstances arising or an event occurring does not, in itself, make those rights protective.**\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_3\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"principal_agent_assessment\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Analyse principal-agent\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Cette approche s’applique lorsque les droits de décision sur les activités pertinentes sont délégués à un décideur. IFRS 10 impose alors de déterminer si ce décideur agit comme principal ou comme agent en appréciant l’étendue de son pouvoir décisionnel, les droits détenus par d’autres parties, sa rémunération et son exposition à la variabilité des rendements (IFRS 10 B58-B60). Si le décideur est un agent, il ne contrôle pas l’entité; si une seule partie peut le révoquer sans motif au titre de droits substantifs, cela suffit en soi à conclure qu’il est agent (IFRS 10 B61, IFRS 10 B65).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Les droits de décision doivent être délégués à un gestionnaire ou autre décideur.\\\",\\n        \\\"Il faut apprécier si ce décideur agit pour son propre compte ou principalement pour le compte d’autres parties.\\\",\\n        \\\"Les droits de révocation ou de restriction détenus par d’autres parties doivent être substantifs pour peser dans l’analyse.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Il faut examiner le mandat, les droits de révocation, les restrictions imposées, la rémunération et les autres intérêts économiques du décideur.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B58\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"When an investor with decision‑making rights (a decision maker) assesses whether it controls an investee, it shall determine whether it is **a principal or an agent**. An investor shall also determine whether another entity with decision‑making rights is acting as an agent for the investor. **An agent is a party primarily engaged to act on behalf and for the benefit of another party or parties (the principal(s)) and therefore does not control the investee** when it exercises its decision‑making authority (see paragraphs 17 and 18). Thus, sometimes a principal’s power may be held and exercisable by an agent, but on behalf of the principal. A decision maker is not an agent simply because other parties can benefit from the decisions that it makes.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B60\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A decision maker shall consider the overall relationship between itself, the investee being managed and other parties involved with the investee, in particular all the factors below, in determining whether it is an agent:\\\\n(a)the scope of its decision‑making authority over the investee (paragraphs B62 and B63).\\\\n(b)the rights held by other parties (paragraphs B64⁠–⁠B67).\\\\n(c)the remuneration to which it is entitled in accordance with the remuneration agreement(s) (paragraphs B68⁠–⁠B70).\\\\n(d)the decision maker’s exposure to variability of returns from other interests that it holds in the investee (paragraphs B71 and B72).\\\\nDifferent weightings shall be applied to each of the factors on the basis of particular facts and circumstances.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B61\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Determining whether a decision maker is an agent requires an evaluation of all the factors listed in paragraph B60 **unless a single party holds substantive rights to remove the decision maker (removal rights) and can remove the decision maker without cause** (see paragraph B65).\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B65\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"When a single party holds **substantive removal rights and can remove the decision maker without cause, this, in isolation, is sufficient to conclude that the decision maker is an agent**. If more than one party holds such rights (and no individual party can remove the decision maker without the agreement of other parties) those rights are not, in isolation, conclusive in determining that a decision maker acts primarily on behalf and for the benefit of others. In addition, the greater the number of parties required to act together to exercise rights to remove a decision maker and the greater the magnitude of, and variability associated with, the decision maker’s other economic interests (ie remuneration and other interests), the less the weighting that shall be placed on this factor.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_4\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"de_facto_power\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Pouvoir de fait\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Cette approche s’applique si, même sans droit explicite suffisant en apparence, l’investisseur a en pratique la capacité de diriger unilatéralement les activités pertinentes. IFRS 10 demande alors de considérer les indices de capacité pratique, les relations particulières avec l’entité et l’existence éventuelle d’agents de fait (IFRS 10 B18-B21, IFRS 10 B73-B75). Cette approche est particulièrement pertinente lorsque les droits formels ne sont pas conclusifs mais que les faits montrent que d’autres parties agissent pour le compte de l’investisseur.\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Les droits formels seuls ne permettent pas de conclure clairement.\\\",\\n        \\\"Les faits doivent montrer une capacité pratique de direction unilatérale ou l’intervention d’agents de fait.\\\",\\n        \\\"Les relations avec d’autres parties doivent être suffisamment étroites pour indiquer qu’elles agissent pour le compte de l’investisseur.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Il faut analyser la capacité réelle à nommer les dirigeants, influencer les transactions, dominer les votes ou diriger des parties liées ou dépendantes.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B18\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In some circumstances it may be difficult to determine whether an investor’s rights are sufficient to give it power over an investee. In such cases, to enable the assessment of power to be made, the investor shall consider evidence of whether it has **the practical ability to direct the relevant activities unilaterally**. Consideration is given, but is not limited, to the following, which, when considered together with its rights and the indicators in paragraphs B19 and B20, may provide evidence that the investor’s rights are sufficient to give it power over the investee:\\\\n(a)The investor can, without having the contractual right to do so, appoint or approve the investee’s key management personnel who have the ability to direct the relevant activities.\\\\n(b)The investor can, without having the contractual right to do so, direct the investee to enter into, or can veto any changes to, significant transactions for the benefit of the investor.\\\\n(c)The investor can dominate either the nominations process for electing members of the investee’s governing body or the obtaining of proxies from other holders of voting rights.\\\\n(d)The investee’s key management personnel are related parties of the investor (for example, the chief executive officer of the investee and the chief executive officer of the investor are the same person).\\\\n(e)The majority of the members of the investee’s governing body are related parties of the investor.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B19\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Sometimes there will be indications that the investor has a special relationship with the investee, which suggests that the investor has more than a passive interest in the investee. The existence of any individual indicator, or a particular combination of indicators, does not necessarily mean that the power criterion is met. However, having more than a passive interest in the investee may indicate that the investor has other related rights sufficient to give it power or provide evidence of existing power over an investee. For example, the following suggests that the investor has more than a passive interest in the investee and, in combination with other rights, may indicate power:\\\\n(a)The investee’s key management personnel who have the ability to direct the relevant activities are current or previous employees of the investor.\\\\n(b)The investee’s operations are dependent on the investor, such as in the following situations:\\\\n(i)The investee depends on the investor to fund a significant portion of its operations.\\\\n(ii)The investor guarantees a significant portion of the investee’s obligations.\\\\n(iii)The investee depends on the investor for critical services, technology, supplies or raw materials.\\\\n(iv)The investor controls assets such as licences or trademarks that are critical to the investee’s operations.\\\\n(v)The investee depends on the investor for key management personnel, such as when the investor’s personnel have specialised knowledge of the investee’s operations.\\\\n(c)A significant portion of the investee’s activities either involve or are conducted on behalf of the investor.\\\\n(d)The investor’s exposure, or rights, to returns from its involvement with the investee is disproportionately greater than its voting or other similar rights. For example, there may be a situation in which an investor is entitled, or exposed, to more than half of the returns of the investee but holds less than half of the voting rights of the investee.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B20\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"The greater an investor’s exposure, or rights, to variability of returns from its involvement with an investee, the greater is the incentive for the investor to obtain rights sufficient to give it power. Therefore, having a large exposure to variability of returns is an indicator that the investor may have power. However, **the extent of the investor’s exposure does not, in itself, determine whether an investor has power over the investee**.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B21\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"When the factors set out in paragraph B18 and the indicators set out in paragraphs B19 and B20 are considered together with an investor’s rights, **greater weight shall be given to the evidence of power described in paragraph B18**.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B73\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"When assessing control, an investor shall consider the nature of its relationship with other parties and whether those other parties are acting on the investor’s behalf (ie they are ‘**de facto agents**’). The determination of whether other parties are acting as de facto agents requires judgement, considering not only the nature of the relationship but also how those parties interact with each other and the investor.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B74\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Such a relationship need not involve a contractual arrangement. A party is a de facto agent when the investor has the ability to direct that party to act on the investor’s behalf. A party might also be a de facto agent when those that direct the activities of the investor have the ability to direct that party to act on the investor’s behalf. The investor shall consider **its de facto agent’s decision‑making rights and its indirect exposure, or rights, to variable returns through the de facto agent together with its own** when assessing control of an investee.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B75\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"The following are examples of such other parties that, by the nature of their relationship, **might act as de facto agents for the investor**:\\\\n(a)the investor’s related parties.\\\\n(b)a party that received its interest in the investee as a contribution or loan from the investor.\\\\n(c)a party that has agreed not to sell, transfer or encumber its interests in the investee without the investor’s prior approval (except for situations in which the investor and the other party have the right of prior approval and the rights are based on mutually agreed terms by willing independent parties).\\\\n(d)a party that cannot finance its operations without subordinated financial support from the investor.\\\\n(e)an investee for which the majority of the members of its governing body or for which its key management personnel are the same as those of the investor.\\\\n(f)a party that has a close business relationship with the investor, such as the relationship between a professional service provider and one of its significant clients.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    }\\n  ]\\n}\"}","expires":1778935911841}

cache: keyv:repeat:0:exec:uv run python /Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/scripts/run_answer.py:4606dc2dc0f5c27588a548eb91f4dfc4f2d83a6b2066151c169a0cb3228e57d0:Quels éléments permettent d’établir qu’un investisseur détient le pouvoir sur une entité au sens d’IFRS 10 ?:{"id":"exec:uv run python /Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/scripts/run_answer.py","config":{"policy-config":"./effective/policy.default.yaml","llm_provider":"openai-codex","basePath":"/Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/experiments/57_answer-evals_on_variants_for_Q5_Q6_Q9_Q12_Q16/runs/2026-05-02_12-49-24_promptfoo-eval-family-q6"},"env":{}}

cache.value: {"value":"{\"output\":\"{\\n  \\\"assumptions_fr\\\": [\\n    \\\"La question est formulée de manière générale et ne décrit pas un cas factuel précis d’investisseur ou d’entité.\\\",\\n    \\\"La réponse vise uniquement les éléments permettant d’établir le pouvoir au sens d’IFRS 10, c’est-à-dire la capacité actuelle de diriger les activités pertinentes de l’entité.\\\",\\n    \\\"Les éléments de pouvoir sont analysés dans le cadre plus large du contrôle IFRS 10, sans traiter ici de manière exhaustive les deux autres critères du contrôle que sont l’exposition aux rendements variables et le lien entre pouvoir et rendements.\\\"\\n  ],\\n  \\\"recommendation\\\": {\\n    \\\"answer\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n    \\\"justification\\\": \\\"Oui, IFRS 10 identifie plusieurs bases permettant d’établir le pouvoir, mais leur application dépend des faits: activités pertinentes, droits substantifs, absence de simples droits protectifs, et capacité actuelle de direction. Il faut donc apprécier chaque voie à la lumière de la structure concrète de l’entité.\\\"\\n  },\\n  \\\"approaches\\\": [\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_1\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"voting_rights\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Droits de vote\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Cette voie est pertinente si, dans la situation analysée, les activités pertinentes sont dirigées par vote et si les droits de vote donnent à l’investisseur la capacité actuelle de les diriger. IFRS 10 précise qu’un détenteur de la majorité a en principe le pouvoir, sous réserve que ses droits soient substantifs et qu’aucun autre droit existant ne retire cette capacité (ifrs10 B35, B36). Même sans majorité, le pouvoir peut exister si l’investisseur a la capacité pratique de diriger unilatéralement les activités pertinentes compte tenu de la dispersion des autres porteurs et des autres faits et circonstances (ifrs10 B41, B42, B43, B45).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Les activités pertinentes doivent être dirigées par les droits de vote.\\\",\\n        \\\"Les droits de vote de l’investisseur doivent être substantifs.\\\",\\n        \\\"Aucun autre titulaire ne doit disposer de droits existants lui donnant la capacité actuelle de diriger les activités pertinentes.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Il faut analyser la majorité, la dispersion de l’actionnariat, les accords entre actionnaires et les schémas de vote effectifs.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B35\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"An investor that holds more than half of the voting rights of an investee has power in the following situations, unless paragraph B36 or paragraph B37 applies: **(a)the relevant activities are directed by a vote of the holder of the majority of the voting rights, or (b)a majority of the members of the governing body that directs the relevant activities are appointed by a vote of the holder of the majority of the voting rights.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B36\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"For an investor that holds more than half of the voting rights of an investee, to have power over an investee, **the investor’s voting rights must be substantive, in accordance with paragraphs B22⁠–⁠B25, and must provide the investor with the current ability to direct the relevant activities**, which often will be through determining operating and financing policies. **If another entity has existing rights that provide that entity with the right to direct the relevant activities and that entity is not an agent of the investor, the investor does not have power over the investee.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B41\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"**An investor with less than a majority of the voting rights has rights that are sufficient to give it power when the investor has the practical ability to direct the relevant activities unilaterally.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B42\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"When assessing whether an investor’s voting rights are sufficient to give it power, an investor considers all facts and circumstances, including: **(a)the size of the investor’s holding of voting rights relative to the size and dispersion of holdings of the other vote holders** ... **(b)potential voting rights held by the investor, other vote holders or other parties** ... **(c)rights arising from other contractual arrangements** ... and **(d)any additional facts and circumstances that indicate the investor has, or does not have, the current ability to direct the relevant activities at the time that decisions need to be made**, including voting patterns at previous shareholders’ meetings.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B43\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"When the direction of relevant activities is determined by majority vote and **an investor holds significantly more voting rights than any other vote holder or organised group of vote holders, and the other shareholdings are widely dispersed**, it may be clear, after considering the factors listed in paragraph B42(a)⁠–⁠(c) alone, that **the investor has power over the investee.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B45\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"However, the factors listed in paragraph B42(a)⁠–⁠(c) alone may not be conclusive. If an investor, having considered those factors, is unclear whether it has power, **it shall consider additional facts and circumstances, such as whether other shareholders are passive in nature as demonstrated by voting patterns at previous shareholders’ meetings.** This includes the assessment of the factors set out in paragraph B18 and the indicators in paragraphs B19 and B20.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_2\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"contractual_decision_making_rights\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Droits contractuels de décision\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Cette approche s’applique lorsque les droits de vote ne sont pas le facteur déterminant ou ne concernent que des tâches administratives, et que des accords contractuels donnent la capacité actuelle de diriger les activités pertinentes. IFRS 10 retient expressément les droits d’appointement, de révocation, de direction ou de veto sur des transactions significatives comme sources possibles de pouvoir (ifrs10 10, B15, B17). L’analyse doit aussi tenir compte de la conception de l’entité et des droits intégrés à sa structure ou à des contrats étroitement liés à ses activités (ifrs10 B51, B52, B53).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Les accords contractuels doivent conférer des droits existants sur les activités pertinentes.\\\",\\n        \\\"Ces droits doivent être substantifs et non simplement protectifs.\\\",\\n        \\\"Les droits de vote ne doivent pas être, en substance, le facteur prédominant de direction des activités pertinentes.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Il faut lire les statuts, pactes, contrats de gestion, clauses de veto et droits de nomination/remplacement des décideurs.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"10\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"An investor has power over an investee when **the investor has existing rights that give it the current ability to direct the relevant activities**, ie the activities that significantly affect the investee’s returns.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B15\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Examples of rights that, either individually or in combination, can give an investor power include but are not limited to: **(b)rights to appoint, reassign or remove members of an investee’s key management personnel who have the ability to direct the relevant activities ; (c)rights to appoint or remove another entity that directs the relevant activities; (d)rights to direct the investee to enter into, or veto any changes to, transactions for the benefit of the investor; and (e)other rights (such as decision‑making rights specified in a management contract) that give the holder the ability to direct the relevant activities.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B17\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"When voting rights cannot have a significant effect on an investee’s returns, such as when voting rights relate to administrative tasks only and **contractual arrangements determine the direction of the relevant activities**, the investor needs to assess those contractual arrangements in order to determine whether it has rights sufficient to give it power over the investee.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B51\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In assessing the purpose and design of an investee (see paragraphs B5⁠–⁠B8), an investor shall consider the involvement and decisions made at the investee’s inception as part of its design and evaluate whether **the transaction terms and features of the involvement provide the investor with rights that are sufficient to give it power.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B52\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In addition, an investor shall consider contractual arrangements such as call rights, put rights and liquidation rights established at the investee’s inception. **When these contractual arrangements involve activities that are closely related to the investee, then these activities are, in substance, an integral part of the investee’s overall activities** ... **decision‑making rights embedded in contractual arrangements that are closely related to the investee need to be considered as relevant activities when determining power over the investee.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B53\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"For some investees, relevant activities occur only when particular circumstances arise or events occur. The investee may be designed so that the direction of its activities and its returns are predetermined unless and until those particular circumstances arise or events occur. In this case, **only the decisions about the investee’s activities when those circumstances or events occur can significantly affect its returns and thus be relevant activities.** The circumstances or events need not have occurred for an investor with the ability to make those decisions to have power.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_3\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"substantive_potential_voting_rights\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Droits de vote potentiels substantifs\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Cette voie est applicable si l’investisseur détient des options, instruments convertibles ou forwards qui lui donnent, seuls ou avec d’autres droits, la capacité actuelle de diriger les activités pertinentes. IFRS 10 impose cependant de ne considérer les droits potentiels que s’ils sont substantifs, ce qui suppose notamment une capacité pratique d’exercice au moment où les décisions doivent être prises (ifrs10 B47, B50, B23, B24). L’analyse inclut aussi l’objet et les caractéristiques de l’instrument et de l’implication globale de l’investisseur (ifrs10 B48, B49).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Les droits potentiels doivent être substantifs.\\\",\\n        \\\"Ils doivent être exercables de manière à donner une capacité actuelle de direction lorsque les décisions pertinentes doivent être prises.\\\",\\n        \\\"L’exercice doit procurer à l’investisseur, seul ou avec d’autres droits, la capacité de diriger les activités pertinentes.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Il faut apprécier les prix d’exercice, les délais d’exercice, les barrières pratiques et le lien avec les autres droits existants.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B47\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"When assessing control, an investor considers its potential voting rights as well as potential voting rights held by other parties, to determine whether it has power. Potential voting rights are rights to obtain voting rights of an investee, such as those arising from convertible instruments or options, including forward contracts. **Those potential voting rights are considered only if the rights are substantive** (see paragraphs B22⁠–⁠B25).\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B48\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"When considering potential voting rights, an investor shall consider **the purpose and design of the instrument, as well as the purpose and design of any other involvement the investor has with the investee.** This includes an assessment of the various terms and conditions of the instrument as well as the investor’s apparent expectations, motives and reasons for agreeing to those terms and conditions.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B49\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If the investor also has voting or other decision‑making rights relating to the investee’s activities, **the investor assesses whether those rights, in combination with potential voting rights, give the investor power.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B50\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"**Substantive potential voting rights alone, or in combination with other rights, can give an investor the current ability to direct the relevant activities.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B23\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Determining whether rights are substantive requires judgement, taking into account all facts and circumstances. Factors to consider in making that determination include but are not limited to: **(a)Whether there are any barriers (economic or otherwise) that prevent the holder (or holders) from exercising the rights** ... **(c)Whether the party or parties that hold the rights would benefit from the exercise of those rights.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B24\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"To be substantive, rights also need to be **exercisable when decisions about the direction of the relevant activities need to be made. Usually, to be substantive, the rights need to be currently exercisable.** However, sometimes rights can be substantive, even though the rights are not currently exercisable.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_4\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"principal_decision_maker\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Décideur agissant comme principal\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Cette approche est pertinente lorsque le pouvoir de décision a été délégué à un gestionnaire ou autre décideur. IFRS 10 exige alors d’apprécier si ce décideur agit comme principal ou comme agent; seul un principal peut contrôler l’entité via ses droits décisionnels (ifrs10 B58, B60, B61). L’évaluation repose sur l’étendue du pouvoir discrétionnaire, les droits détenus par d’autres parties, la rémunération et l’exposition du décideur à la variabilité des rendements (ifrs10 B62, B64, B65, B71, B72).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Il doit exister des droits décisionnels délégués à l’investisseur ou à un autre décideur.\\\",\\n        \\\"Le décideur doit être qualifié de principal et non d’agent.\\\",\\n        \\\"Les droits de retrait ou de restriction détenus par d’autres parties ne doivent pas conduire à conclure que le décideur agit principalement pour autrui.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Il faut analyser les contrats de gestion, les droits de révocation sans motif, la structure des commissions et les intérêts économiques du gestionnaire.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B58\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"When an investor with decision‑making rights (a decision maker) assesses whether it controls an investee, **it shall determine whether it is a principal or an agent.** An investor shall also determine whether another entity with decision‑making rights is acting as an agent for the investor. **An agent is a party primarily engaged to act on behalf and for the benefit of another party or parties (the principal(s)) and therefore does not control the investee when it exercises its decision‑making authority** (see paragraphs 17 and 18).\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B60\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A decision maker shall consider the overall relationship between itself, the investee being managed and other parties involved with the investee, in particular all the factors below, in determining whether it is an agent: **(a)the scope of its decision‑making authority over the investee** ... **(b)the rights held by other parties** ... **(c)the remuneration to which it is entitled** ... **(d)the decision maker’s exposure to variability of returns from other interests that it holds in the investee** ...\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B61\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Determining whether a decision maker is an agent requires an evaluation of all the factors listed in paragraph B60 **unless a single party holds substantive rights to remove the decision maker (removal rights) and can remove the decision maker without cause** (see paragraph B65).\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B62\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"The scope of a decision maker ’s decision‑making authority is evaluated by considering: **(a)the activities that are permitted according to the decision‑making agreement(s) and specified by law, and (b)the discretion that the decision maker has when making decisions about those activities.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B64\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"**Substantive rights held by other parties may affect the decision maker ’s ability to direct the relevant activities of an investee. Substantive removal or other rights may indicate that the decision maker is an agent.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B65\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"**When a single party holds substantive removal rights and can remove the decision maker without cause, this, in isolation, is sufficient to conclude that the decision maker is an agent.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B71\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A decision maker that holds other interests in an investee (eg investments in the investee or provides guarantees with respect to the performance of the investee), shall consider **its exposure to variability of returns from those interests in assessing whether it is an agent. Holding other interests in an investee indicates that the decision maker may be a principal.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B72\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In evaluating its exposure to variability of returns from other interests in the investee a decision maker shall consider the following: **(a)the greater the magnitude of, and variability associated with, its economic interests, considering its remuneration and other interests in aggregate, the more likely the decision maker is a principal.**\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_5\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"practical_ability_and_de_facto_agency\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Capacité pratique et agent de fait\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Cette approche complète l’analyse lorsque les droits formels ne suffisent pas, à eux seuls, à rendre la conclusion évidente. IFRS 10 demande alors d’examiner les indices de capacité pratique à diriger unilatéralement l’entité et les relations spéciales avec l’investee, tout en donnant davantage de poids aux preuves directes de pouvoir qu’aux simples indicateurs (ifrs10 B18 à B21). L’investisseur doit aussi tenir compte des droits et expositions détenus via des agents de fait, c’est-à-dire des parties qu’il peut diriger pour agir en son nom (ifrs10 B73, B74, B75).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Il doit exister, en plus des droits formels, des éléments probants montrant une capacité pratique de direction unilatérale.\\\",\\n        \\\"Les relations avec d’autres parties doivent démontrer qu’elles agissent pour le compte de l’investisseur.\\\",\\n        \\\"Ces indices doivent être appréciés avec les droits existants; ils ne suffisent pas isolément s’ils ne corroborent pas le pouvoir.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Il faut analyser les nominations de dirigeants, dépendances opérationnelles, accords de financement, parties liées et détentions indirectes.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B18\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In some circumstances it may be difficult to determine whether an investor’s rights are sufficient to give it power over an investee. In such cases, to enable the assessment of power to be made, **the investor shall consider evidence of whether it has the practical ability to direct the relevant activities unilaterally.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B19\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Sometimes there will be indications that the investor has a special relationship with the investee, which suggests that **the investor has more than a passive interest in the investee.** The existence of any individual indicator, or a particular combination of indicators, does not necessarily mean that the power criterion is met. However, having more than a passive interest in the investee may indicate that the investor has other related rights sufficient to give it power or provide evidence of existing power over an investee.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B20\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"The greater an investor’s exposure, or rights, to variability of returns from its involvement with an investee, **the greater is the incentive for the investor to obtain rights sufficient to give it power. Therefore, having a large exposure to variability of returns is an indicator that the investor may have power. However, the extent of the investor’s exposure does not, in itself, determine whether an investor has power over the investee.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B21\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"When the factors set out in paragraph B18 and the indicators set out in paragraphs B19 and B20 are considered together with an investor’s rights, **greater weight shall be given to the evidence of power described in paragraph B18.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B73\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"When assessing control, an investor shall consider the nature of its relationship with other parties and whether those other parties are acting on the investor’s behalf (ie they are ‘de facto agents’). **The determination of whether other parties are acting as de facto agents requires judgement**, considering not only the nature of the relationship but also how those parties interact with each other and the investor.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B74\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Such a relationship need not involve a contractual arrangement. **A party is a de facto agent when the investor has the ability to direct that party to act on the investor’s behalf.** ... **The investor shall consider its de facto agent’s decision‑making rights and its indirect exposure, or rights, to variable returns through the de facto agent together with its own when assessing control of an investee.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B75\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"The following are examples of such other parties that, by the nature of their relationship, **might act as de facto agents for the investor**: **(a)the investor’s related parties** ... **(b)a party that received its interest in the investee as a contribution or loan from the investor** ... **(d)a party that cannot finance its operations without subordinated financial support from the investor** ...\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_6\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"deemed_separate_entity\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Entité séparée réputée (silo)\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Cette approche n’est pertinente que si la question porte sur une portion d’entité dont les actifs, passifs et flux sont, en substance, cantonnés et isolés du reste de l’investee. IFRS 10 exige alors d’apprécier séparément le pouvoir sur ce silo, en identifiant les activités qui affectent significativement ses rendements et la manière dont elles sont dirigées (ifrs10 B76 à B79). En l’absence d’un tel cantonnement économique et juridique, cette voie ne s’applique pas.\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Une portion de l’entité doit satisfaire à la condition de cantonnement prévue pour un silo.\\\",\\n        \\\"Les actifs spécifiés doivent être la seule source de paiement de passifs ou intérêts spécifiés liés à cette portion.\\\",\\n        \\\"L’analyse doit viser le pouvoir sur cette portion distincte et non sur l’entité globale.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Il faut d’abord vérifier l’existence d’un silo, puis réaliser un test de pouvoir spécifique sur cette portion isolée.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B76\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"**An investor shall consider whether it treats a portion of an investee as a deemed separate entity and, if so, whether it controls the deemed separate entity.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B77\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"An investor shall treat a portion of an investee as a deemed separate entity **if and only if** the following condition is satisfied: **Specified assets of the investee (and related credit enhancements, if any) are the only source of payment for specified liabilities of, or specified other interests in, the investee.** ... **Thus, in substance, all the assets, liabilities and equity of that deemed separate entity are ring‑fenced from the overall investee. Such a deemed separate entity is often called a ‘silo’.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B78\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"When the condition in paragraph B77 is satisfied, **an investor shall identify the activities that significantly affect the returns of the deemed separate entity and how those activities are directed in order to assess whether it has power over that portion of the investee.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B79\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"**If the investor controls the deemed separate entity, the investor shall consolidate that portion of the investee.** In that case, other parties exclude that portion of the investee when assessing control of, and in consolidating, the investee.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    }\\n  ]\\n}\"}","expires":1778935931996}

Comment déterminer si ces droits lui confèrent le pouvoir pertinent pour conclure à un contrôle ?:{"id":"exec:uv run python /Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/scripts/run_answer.py","config":{"policy-config":"./effective/policy.default.yaml","llm_provider":"openai-codex","basePath":"/Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/experiments/57_answer-evals_on_variants_for_Q5_Q6_Q9_Q12_Q16/runs/2026-05-02_12-49-24_promptfoo-eval-family-q6"},"env":{}}

cache.value: {"value":"{\"output\":\"{\\n  \\\"assumptions_fr\\\": [\\n    \\\"La question vise une appréciation IFRS 10 du contrôle d’une entité par un investisseur, sans autre norme concurrente à appliquer.\\\",\\n    \\\"Les faits fournis ne permettent pas d’identifier avec certitude si le pouvoir provient de droits de vote, de clauses contractuelles ou de droits délégués; les trois approches identifiées doivent donc être testées.\\\",\\n    \\\"L’analyse porte sur le pouvoir pertinent au moment de l’évaluation, en tenant compte de tous les faits et circonstances disponibles.\\\"\\n  ],\\n  \\\"recommendation\\\": {\\n    \\\"answer\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n    \\\"justification\\\": \\\"Oui, si les droits analysés sont substantifs, non protectifs, et donnent à l’investisseur la capacité actuelle de diriger les activités pertinentes de l’entité, avec exposition à des rendements variables et lien entre pouvoir et rendements (IFRS 10 6, 7, B9). En l’absence de précisions factuelles, la conclusion dépend de la source réelle du pouvoir: vote, contrat ou droits délégués.\\\"\\n  },\\n  \\\"approaches\\\": [\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_1\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"voting_rights_power\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Pouvoir par droits de vote\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Cette approche s’applique dans cette situation seulement si les droits exercés sont des droits de vote — actuels ou potentiels substantifs — permettant à l’investisseur de diriger unilatéralement les activités pertinentes (IFRS 10 B34, B41, B47). Si l’entité est gouvernée par vote, il faut apprécier la taille relative de la participation, la dispersion des autres porteurs et les autres droits disponibles; sinon, les droits de vote ne suffisent pas (IFRS 10 B42, B46).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Les activités pertinentes de l’entité sont dirigées par vote ou droits similaires.\\\",\\n        \\\"Les droits de vote ou droits potentiels sont substantifs et donnent une capacité actuelle de direction.\\\",\\n        \\\"Les autres porteurs ne peuvent pas, en pratique, empêcher l’investisseur de diriger unilatéralement les activités pertinentes.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"L’investisseur conclut au pouvoir seulement si ses droits de vote, seuls ou combinés à des droits substantifs, lui donnent concrètement la capacité actuelle de diriger les activités pertinentes.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B34\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Often an investor has the current ability, through voting or similar rights, to direct the relevant activities. **An investor considers the requirements in this section (paragraphs B35⁠–⁠B50) if the relevant activities of an investee are directed through voting rights.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B41\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"**An investor with less than a majority of the voting rights has rights that are sufficient to give it power when the investor has the practical ability to direct the relevant activities unilaterally.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B42\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"When assessing whether an investor’s voting rights are sufficient to give it power, **an investor considers all facts and circumstances**, including:\\\\n(a)the size of the investor’s holding of voting rights relative to the size and dispersion of holdings of the other vote holders, noting that:\\\\n(i)the more voting rights an investor holds, the more likely the investor is to have existing rights that give it the current ability to direct the relevant activities ;\\\\n(ii)the more voting rights an investor holds relative to other vote holders, the more likely the investor is to have existing rights that give it the current ability to direct the relevant activities;\\\\n(iii)the more parties that would need to act together to outvote the investor, the more likely the investor is to have existing rights that give it the current ability to direct the relevant activities;\\\\n(b)potential voting rights held by the investor, other vote holders or other parties (see paragraphs B47⁠–⁠B50);\\\\n(c)rights arising from other contractual arrangements (see paragraph B40); and\\\\n(d)any additional facts and circumstances that indicate the investor has, or does not have, the current ability to direct the relevant activities at the time that decisions need to be made, including voting patterns at previous shareholders’ meetings.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B46\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"**If it is not clear, having considered the factors listed in paragraph B42(a)⁠–⁠(d), that the investor has power, the investor does not control the investee.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B47\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"When assessing control, **an investor considers its potential voting rights as well as potential voting rights held by other parties, to determine whether it has power**. Potential voting rights are rights to obtain voting rights of an investee, such as those arising from convertible instruments or options, including forward contracts. **Those potential voting rights are considered only if the rights are substantive** (see paragraphs B22⁠–⁠B25).\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_2\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"contractual_power\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Pouvoir par droits contractuels\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Cette approche est pertinente ici si la portée des droits doit être analysée au-delà du vote, notamment lorsque l’entité est conçue de sorte que les activités pertinentes sont dirigées par contrat (IFRS 10 11, B17). Il faut alors identifier les activités pertinentes, vérifier que les droits sont substantifs et non simplement protectifs, et déterminer s’ils donnent la capacité actuelle de les diriger (IFRS 10 10, B9, B22, B26).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Les droits de vote ne sont pas le facteur dominant ou ne portent que sur des tâches administratives.\\\",\\n        \\\"Les droits contractuels concernent les activités qui affectent significativement les rendements de l’entité.\\\",\\n        \\\"Ces droits sont substantifs et ne sont pas seulement protectifs.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"L’investisseur contrôle l’entité si ses clauses contractuelles lui donnent effectivement la capacité actuelle de diriger les activités pertinentes, même sans majorité de vote.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"11\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Power arises from rights. Sometimes assessing power is straightforward, such as when power over an investee is obtained directly and solely from the voting rights granted by equity instruments such as shares, and can be assessed by considering the voting rights from those shareholdings. **In other cases, the assessment will be more complex and require more than one factor to be considered, for example when power results from one or more contractual arrangements.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"10\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"**An investor has power over an investee when the investor has existing rights that give it the current ability to direct the relevant activities**, ie the activities that significantly affect the investee’s returns. E4\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B17\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"When voting rights cannot have a significant effect on an investee’s returns, such as when voting rights relate to administrative tasks only and **contractual arrangements determine the direction of the relevant activities**, the investor needs to assess those contractual arrangements in order to determine whether it has rights sufficient to give it power over the investee. To determine whether an investor has rights sufficient to give it power, the investor shall consider the purpose and design of the investee (see paragraphs B5⁠–⁠B8) and the requirements in paragraphs B51⁠–⁠B54 together with paragraphs B18⁠–⁠B20.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B9\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"To have power over an investee, **an investor must have existing rights that give it the current ability to direct the relevant activities**. For the purpose of assessing power, **only substantive rights and rights that are not protective shall be considered** (see paragraphs B22⁠–⁠B28).\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B22\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"**An investor, in assessing whether it has power, considers only substantive rights relating to an investee** (held by the investor and others). **For a right to be substantive, the holder must have the practical ability to exercise that right.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B26\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In evaluating whether rights give an investor power over an investee, the investor shall assess whether its rights, and rights held by others, are protective rights. **Protective rights relate to fundamental changes to the activities of an investee or apply in exceptional circumstances.** However, not all rights that apply in exceptional circumstances or are contingent on events are protective (see paragraphs B13 and B53). E12\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_3\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"principal_power\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Pouvoir en tant que principal\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Cette approche ne s’applique dans cette situation que si l’investisseur est un décisionnaire exerçant des droits délégués pour l’entité. Il faut alors déterminer s’il agit comme principal ou comme agent en appréciant l’étendue de son pouvoir, les droits de retrait ou de limitation détenus par d’autres, sa rémunération et son exposition aux rendements variables (IFRS 10 B58, B60, B61).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Les droits exercés résultent d’une délégation de pouvoir décisionnel sur tout ou partie des activités pertinentes.\\\",\\n        \\\"L’investisseur n’est pas révocable sans cause par une seule partie détenant des droits substantifs.\\\",\\n        \\\"L’analyse globale montre qu’il agit principalement pour son propre compte et non comme agent.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si l’investisseur est principal, ses droits délégués sont traités comme un pouvoir de contrôle; s’il est agent, il ne contrôle pas l’entité au titre de ces droits.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B58\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"When an investor with decision‑making rights (a decision maker) assesses whether it controls an investee, **it shall determine whether it is a principal or an agent**. An investor shall also determine whether another entity with decision‑making rights is acting as an agent for the investor. **An agent is a party primarily engaged to act on behalf and for the benefit of another party or parties (the principal(s)) and therefore does not control the investee when it exercises its decision‑making authority** (see paragraphs 17 and 18). Thus, sometimes a principal’s power may be held and exercisable by an agent, but on behalf of the principal. A decision maker is not an agent simply because other parties can benefit from the decisions that it makes.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B60\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A decision maker shall consider the overall relationship between itself, the investee being managed and other parties involved with the investee, in particular all the factors below, in determining whether it is an agent:\\\\n(a)the scope of its decision‑making authority over the investee (paragraphs B62 and B63).\\\\n(b)the rights held by other parties (paragraphs B64⁠–⁠B67).\\\\n(c)the remuneration to which it is entitled in accordance with the remuneration agreement(s) (paragraphs B68⁠–⁠B70).\\\\n(d)the decision maker’s exposure to variability of returns from other interests that it holds in the investee (paragraphs B71 and B72).\\\\n**Different weightings shall be applied to each of the factors on the basis of particular facts and circumstances.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B61\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"**Determining whether a decision maker is an agent requires an evaluation of all the factors listed in paragraph B60 unless a single party holds substantive rights to remove the decision maker (removal rights) and can remove the decision maker without cause** (see paragraph B65).\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    }\\n  ]\\n}\"}","expires":1778936036669}

Comment déterminer si ces droits lui confèrent le pouvoir pertinent pour conclure à un contrôle ?:{"id":"exec:uv run python /Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/scripts/run_answer.py","config":{"policy-config":"./effective/policy.default.yaml","llm_provider":"openai-codex","basePath":"/Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/experiments/57_answer-evals_on_variants_for_Q5_Q6_Q9_Q12_Q16/runs/2026-05-02_12-49-24_promptfoo-eval-family-q6"},"env":{}}

cache.value: {"value":"{\"output\":\"{\\n  \\\"assumptions_fr\\\": [\\n    \\\"On suppose que l’analyse porte sur un investisseur au sens d’IFRS 10 et sur la question de savoir s’il contrôle l’entité au niveau des états financiers consolidés.\\\",\\n    \\\"On suppose que les faits disponibles ne permettent pas d’identifier d’emblée si les droits en cause sont des droits de vote, des droits contractuels ou des droits de décision délégués ; les trois approches doivent donc être examinées.\\\",\\n    \\\"On suppose que la conclusion recherchée est celle du pouvoir pertinent sur les activités qui affectent significativement les rendements de l’entité.\\\"\\n  ],\\n  \\\"recommendation\\\": {\\n    \\\"answer\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n    \\\"justification\\\": \\\"Oui, mais seulement si les droits détenus sont substantifs, non protectifs et donnent la capacité actuelle de diriger les activités pertinentes de l’entité. En pratique, la conclusion dépend de la nature des droits exercés : droits de vote, droits contractuels ou droits décisionnels délégués au titre d’une relation principal-agent.\\\"\\n  },\\n  \\\"approaches\\\": [\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_1\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"power_through_voting_rights\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Pouvoir par les droits de vote\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Cette approche s’applique dans cette situation si les droits exercés sont des droits de vote ou des droits potentiels de vote. IFRS 10 exige d’apprécier si ces droits sont substantifs et donnent la capacité actuelle de diriger les activités pertinentes, y compris avec moins de 50 % des votes si l’investisseur a la capacité pratique de diriger unilatéralement (IFRS 10 B41-B42, B47-B50). Si, au contraire, après examen des faits, il n’est pas clair que ces droits confèrent le pouvoir, l’investisseur ne contrôle pas l’entité (IFRS 10 B46).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Les activités pertinentes doivent être dirigées par vote ou par des droits similaires.\\\",\\n        \\\"Les droits de vote ou droits potentiels de vote doivent être substantifs.\\\",\\n        \\\"Les autres détenteurs de droits ne doivent pas pouvoir empêcher en substance l’investisseur de diriger les activités pertinentes.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Il faut mesurer la taille relative de la participation, la dispersion des autres détenteurs, les accords contractuels et le caractère substantif des droits potentiels avant de conclure au contrôle.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B41\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"An investor with less than a majority of the voting rights has rights that are sufficient to give it power when the investor has **the practical ability to direct the relevant activities unilaterally**.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B42\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"When assessing whether an investor’s voting rights are sufficient to give it power, an investor considers all facts and circumstances, including:\\\\n(a)the size of the investor’s holding of voting rights relative to the size and dispersion of holdings of the other vote holders, noting that:\\\\n(i)the more voting rights an investor holds, the more likely the investor is to have existing rights that give it **the current ability to direct the relevant activities** ;\\\\n(ii)the more voting rights an investor holds relative to other vote holders, the more likely the investor is to have existing rights that give it **the current ability to direct the relevant activities**;\\\\n(iii)the more parties that would need to act together to outvote the investor, the more likely the investor is to have existing rights that give it **the current ability to direct the relevant activities**;\\\\n(b)potential voting rights held by the investor, other vote holders or other parties (see paragraphs B47⁠–⁠B50);\\\\n(c)rights arising from other contractual arrangements (see paragraph B40); and\\\\n(d)any additional facts and circumstances that indicate the investor has, or does not have, **the current ability to direct the relevant activities** at the time that decisions need to be made, including voting patterns at previous shareholders’ meetings.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B46\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If it is not clear, having considered the factors listed in paragraph B42(a)⁠–⁠(d), that the investor has power, **the investor does not control the investee**.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B47\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"When assessing control, an investor considers its potential voting rights as well as potential voting rights held by other parties, to determine whether it has power. Potential voting rights are rights to obtain voting rights of an investee, such as those arising from convertible instruments or options, including forward contracts. Those potential voting rights are considered only if the rights are **substantive** (see paragraphs B22⁠–⁠B25).\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B50\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Substantive potential voting rights alone, or in combination with other rights, can give an investor **the current ability to direct the relevant activities**. For example, this is likely to be the case when an investor holds 40 per cent of the voting rights of an investee and, in accordance with paragraph B23, holds substantive rights arising from options to acquire a further 20 per cent of the voting rights.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_2\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"power_through_contractual_rights\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Pouvoir par des droits contractuels\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Cette approche s’applique dans cette situation si les droits exercés ne relèvent pas principalement du vote mais d’accords contractuels ou d’autres droits donnant la capacité actuelle de diriger les activités pertinentes (IFRS 10 10, 11, B17). L’analyse doit alors porter sur la conception de l’entité, l’identification des activités pertinentes, le caractère substantif des droits et l’absence de droits seulement protectifs, car des droits purement protectifs ne donnent pas le pouvoir (IFRS 10 B22-B28, 14).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Les droits contractuels doivent viser les activités qui affectent significativement les rendements de l’entité.\\\",\\n        \\\"Les droits doivent être substantifs et actuellement exerçables lorsque les décisions doivent être prises.\\\",\\n        \\\"Les droits ne doivent pas être seulement protectifs.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Il faut lire les contrats et la conception de l’entité pour identifier qui peut aujourd’hui diriger les activités pertinentes et écarter les droits seulement protectifs.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"10\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"An investor has power over an investee when the investor has **existing rights that give it the current ability to direct the relevant activities**, ie the activities that significantly affect the investee’s returns. E4\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"11\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Power arises from rights. Sometimes assessing power is straightforward, such as when power over an investee is obtained directly and solely from the voting rights granted by equity instruments such as shares, and can be assessed by considering the voting rights from those shareholdings. In other cases, the assessment will be more complex and require more than one factor to be considered, for example when **power results from one or more contractual arrangements**.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B17\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"When voting rights cannot have a significant effect on an investee’s returns, such as when voting rights relate to administrative tasks only and contractual arrangements determine the direction of the relevant activities, the investor needs to assess those contractual arrangements in order to determine whether it has rights sufficient to give it power over the investee. To determine whether an investor has rights sufficient to give it power, the investor shall consider **the purpose and design of the investee** (see paragraphs B5⁠–⁠B8) and the requirements in paragraphs B51⁠–⁠B54 together with paragraphs B18⁠–⁠B20.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B22\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"An investor, in assessing whether it has power, considers only **substantive rights** relating to an investee (held by the investor and others). For a right to be substantive, the holder must have **the practical ability to exercise that right**.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B24\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"To be substantive, rights also need to be **exercisable when decisions about the direction of the relevant activities need to be made**. Usually, to be substantive, the rights need to be currently exercisable. However, sometimes rights can be substantive, even though the rights are not currently exercisable.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B26\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In evaluating whether rights give an investor power over an investee, the investor shall assess whether its rights, and rights held by others, are **protective rights**. Protective rights relate to fundamental changes to the activities of an investee or apply in exceptional circumstances. However, **not all rights that apply in exceptional circumstances or are contingent on events are protective** (see paragraphs B13 and B53). E12\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B27\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Because protective rights are designed to protect the interests of their holder without giving that party power over the investee to which those rights relate, an investor that holds **only protective rights cannot have power or prevent another party from having power over an investee** (see paragraph 14).\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"14\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"An investor can have power over an investee even if other entities have existing rights that give them the current ability to participate in the direction of the relevant activities, for example when another entity has significant influence. However, an investor that holds **only protective rights does not have power over an investee**, and consequently does not control the investee.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_3\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"power_through_delegated_decision_making_rights\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Pouvoir par des droits décisionnels délégués\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Cette approche s’applique dans cette situation si l’investisseur exerce des droits de décision en qualité de gestionnaire, mandataire ou autre décideur délégué. IFRS 10 impose alors de déterminer s’il agit comme principal ou agent ; un agent n’exerce pas le contrôle lorsqu’il utilise son autorité décisionnelle, alors qu’un principal peut contrôler l’entité (IFRS 10 B58-B61). L’analyse dépend de l’étendue du pouvoir, des droits de retrait détenus par d’autres, de la rémunération et de l’exposition aux rendements variables (IFRS 10 B60, B64-B72).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Les droits exercés doivent résulter d’une délégation de pouvoir décisionnel sur tout ou partie des activités pertinentes.\\\",\\n        \\\"L’investisseur doit être principal et non agent.\\\",\\n        \\\"Il faut tenir compte des droits de révocation substantifs détenus par d’autres parties.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si l’investisseur n’agit qu’au nom et pour le bénéfice d’autres parties, il ne contrôle pas l’entité malgré des droits de décision apparents.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B58\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"When an investor with decision‑making rights (a decision maker) assesses whether it controls an investee, it shall determine whether it is a **principal or an agent**. An investor shall also determine whether another entity with decision‑making rights is acting as an agent for the investor. An agent is a party primarily engaged to act on behalf and for the benefit of another party or parties (the principal(s)) and therefore **does not control the investee when it exercises its decision‑making authority** (see paragraphs 17 and 18). Thus, sometimes a principal’s power may be held and exercisable by an agent, but on behalf of the principal. A decision maker is not an agent simply because other parties can benefit from the decisions that it makes.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B60\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A decision maker shall consider the overall relationship between itself, the investee being managed and other parties involved with the investee, in particular all the factors below, in determining whether it is an agent:\\\\n(a)the scope of its decision‑making authority over the investee (paragraphs B62 and B63).\\\\n(b)the rights held by other parties (paragraphs B64⁠–⁠B67).\\\\n(c)the remuneration to which it is entitled in accordance with the remuneration agreement(s) (paragraphs B68⁠–⁠B70).\\\\n(d)the decision maker’s exposure to variability of returns from other interests that it holds in the investee (paragraphs B71 and B72).\\\\nDifferent weightings shall be applied to each of the factors on the basis of particular facts and circumstances.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B61\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Determining whether a decision maker is an agent requires an evaluation of all the factors listed in paragraph B60 unless a single party holds **substantive rights to remove the decision maker** (removal rights) and can remove the decision maker **without cause** (see paragraph B65).\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B64\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Substantive rights held by other parties may affect the decision maker ’s ability to direct the relevant activities of an investee. **Substantive removal or other rights may indicate that the decision maker is an agent.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B65\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"When a single party holds substantive removal rights and can remove the decision maker without cause, this, in isolation, is sufficient to conclude that the decision maker is **an agent**.
