If more than one party holds such rights (and no individual party can remove the decision maker without the agreement of other parties) those rights are not, in isolation, conclusive in determining that a decision maker acts primarily on behalf and for the benefit of others. In addition, the greater the number of parties required to act together to exercise rights to remove a decision maker and the greater the magnitude of, and variability associated with, the decision maker’s other economic interests (ie remuneration and other interests), the less the weighting that shall be placed on this factor.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B71\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A decision maker that holds other interests in an investee (eg investments in the investee or provides guarantees with respect to the performance of the investee), shall consider its exposure to variability of returns from those interests in assessing whether it is an agent. **Holding other interests in an investee indicates that the decision maker may be a principal.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B72\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In evaluating its exposure to variability of returns from other interests in the investee a decision maker shall consider the following:\\\\n(a)the greater the magnitude of, and variability associated with, its economic interests, considering its remuneration and other interests in aggregate, the more likely the decision maker is **a principal**.\\\\n(b)whether its exposure to variability of returns is different from that of the other investors and, if so, whether this might influence its actions. For example, this might be the case when a decision maker holds subordinated interests in, or provides other forms of credit enhancement to, an investee.\\\\nThe decision maker shall evaluate its exposure relative to the total variability of returns of the investee. This evaluation is made primarily on the basis of returns expected from the activities of the investee but shall not ignore the decision maker’s maximum exposure to variability of returns of the investee through other interests that the decision maker holds.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    }\\n  ]\\n}\"}","expires":1778936040432}

cache: keyv:repeat:1:exec:uv run python /Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/scripts/run_answer.py:4606dc2dc0f5c27588a548eb91f4dfc4f2d83a6b2066151c169a0cb3228e57d0:Quels éléments permettent d’établir qu’un investisseur détient le pouvoir sur une entité au sens d’IFRS 10 ?:{"id":"exec:uv run python /Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/scripts/run_answer.py","config":{"policy-config":"./effective/policy.default.yaml","llm_provider":"openai-codex","basePath":"/Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/experiments/57_answer-evals_on_variants_for_Q5_Q6_Q9_Q12_Q16/runs/2026-05-02_12-49-24_promptfoo-eval-family-q6"},"env":{}}

cache.value: {"value":"{\"output\":\"{\\n  \\\"assumptions_fr\\\": [\\n    \\\"La question est formulée de manière générale et ne décrit pas un cas factuel précis d’investisseur ou d’entité.\\\",\\n    \\\"L’analyse porte uniquement sur l’identification des éléments permettant d’établir le pouvoir au sens d’IFRS 10, et non sur la conclusion complète de contrôle, qui exige aussi l’exposition à des rendements variables et le lien entre pouvoir et rendements.\\\",\\n    \\\"En l’absence de faits spécifiques, chaque approche est appréciée comme une base possible d’établissement du pouvoir dans cette situation générale.\\\"\\n  ],\\n  \\\"recommendation\\\": {\\n    \\\"answer\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n    \\\"justification\\\": \\\"Au sens d’IFRS 10, le pouvoir est établi si l’investisseur détient des droits actuels et substantifs lui donnant la capacité actuelle de diriger les activités pertinentes (IFRS 10 10, B9, B14). En pratique, cela peut résulter de droits de vote, de droits potentiels substantifs, de droits contractuels, d’un pouvoir de fait ou de droits délégués exercés en qualité de principal.\\\"\\n  },\\n  \\\"approaches\\\": [\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_1\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"voting_rights_power\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Pouvoir par droits de vote\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Cette approche s’applique à la question, car IFRS 10 prévoit que le pouvoir découle souvent de droits de vote ou de droits similaires donnant la capacité actuelle de diriger les activités pertinentes (IFRS 10 11, B14, B15, B34). Elle permet d’établir le pouvoir si les droits de vote sont substantifs et confèrent la capacité actuelle de diriger les activités pertinentes, y compris avec majorité ou parfois avec moins de 50 % selon les faits (IFRS 10 B35, B36, B38, B41, B42).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Les droits de vote doivent être substantifs.\\\",\\n        \\\"Les activités pertinentes doivent être dirigées par vote ou par des droits similaires.\\\",\\n        \\\"Les autres droits détenus par des tiers ne doivent pas empêcher la capacité actuelle de diriger les activités pertinentes.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Il faut analyser la part de vote, sa dispersion relative, les accords avec autres porteurs et l’existence de droits concurrents.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"11\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Power arises from rights. Sometimes assessing power is straightforward, such as when power over an investee is obtained directly and solely from the voting rights granted by equity instruments such as shares, and can be assessed by considering **the voting rights from those shareholdings**. In other cases, the assessment will be more complex and require more than one factor to be considered, for example when power results from one or more contractual arrangements.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B14\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"**Power arises from rights. To have power over an investee, an investor must have existing rights that give the investor the current ability to direct the relevant activities.** The rights that may give an investor power can differ between investees.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B15\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Examples of rights that, either individually or in combination, can give an investor power include but are not limited to: **(a)rights in the form of voting rights (or potential voting rights) of an investee** (see paragraphs B34⁠–⁠B50); (b)rights to appoint, reassign or remove members of an investee’s key management personnel who have the ability to direct the relevant activities ; (c)rights to appoint or remove another entity that directs the relevant activities; (d)rights to direct the investee to enter into, or veto any changes to, transactions for the benefit of the investor; and (e)other rights (such as decision‑making rights specified in a management contract) that give the holder the ability to direct the relevant activities.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B34\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Often an investor has the current ability, through **voting or similar rights**, to direct the relevant activities. An investor considers the requirements in this section (paragraphs B35⁠–⁠B50) if the relevant activities of an investee are directed through voting rights.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B35\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"An investor that holds **more than half of the voting rights** of an investee has power in the following situations, unless paragraph B36 or paragraph B37 applies: (a)the relevant activities are directed by a vote of the holder of the majority of the voting rights, or (b)a majority of the members of the governing body that directs the relevant activities are appointed by a vote of the holder of the majority of the voting rights.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B36\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"For an investor that holds more than half of the voting rights of an investee, to have power over an investee, **the investor’s voting rights must be substantive**, in accordance with paragraphs B22⁠–⁠B25, and **must provide the investor with the current ability to direct the relevant activities**, which often will be through determining operating and financing policies. If another entity has existing rights that provide that entity with the right to direct the relevant activities and that entity is not an agent of the investor, the investor does not have power over the investee.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B38\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"An investor can have power even if it holds **less than a majority of the voting rights** of an investee. An investor can have power with less than a majority of the voting rights of an investee, for example, through: (a)a contractual arrangement between the investor and other vote holders (see paragraph B39); (b)rights arising from other contractual arrangements (see paragraph B40); (c)the investor’s voting rights (see paragraphs B41⁠–⁠B45); (d)potential voting rights (see paragraphs B47⁠–⁠B50); or (e)a combination of (a)⁠–⁠(d).\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B41\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"An investor with less than a majority of the voting rights has rights that are sufficient to give it power when the investor has **the practical ability to direct the relevant activities unilaterally**.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B42\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"When assessing whether an investor’s voting rights are sufficient to give it power, an investor considers **all facts and circumstances**, including: (a)the size of the investor’s holding of voting rights relative to the size and dispersion of holdings of the other vote holders, noting that: (i)the more voting rights an investor holds, the more likely the investor is to have existing rights that give it the current ability to direct the relevant activities ; (ii)the more voting rights an investor holds relative to other vote holders, the more likely the investor is to have existing rights that give it the current ability to direct the relevant activities; (iii)the more parties that would need to act together to outvote the investor, the more likely the investor is to have existing rights that give it the current ability to direct the relevant activities; (b)potential voting rights held by the investor, other vote holders or other parties (see paragraphs B47⁠–⁠B50); (c)rights arising from other contractual arrangements (see paragraph B40); and (d)any additional facts and circumstances that indicate the investor has, or does not have, the current ability to direct the relevant activities at the time that decisions need to be made, including voting patterns at previous shareholders’ meetings.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_2\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"potential_voting_rights_power\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Pouvoir par droits de vote potentiels\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Cette approche s’applique car IFRS 10 exige de prendre en compte les droits de vote potentiels lorsqu’ils sont substantifs pour apprécier le pouvoir (IFRS 10 B47, B50). Dans la situation générale posée, ces droits peuvent, seuls ou combinés à d’autres droits, donner la capacité actuelle de diriger les activités pertinentes si leur exercice est pratiquement possible et économiquement réel (IFRS 10 B23, B47, B49, B50).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Les droits de vote potentiels doivent être substantifs.\\\",\\n        \\\"Ils doivent donner, seuls ou avec d’autres droits, la capacité actuelle de diriger les activités pertinentes.\\\",\\n        \\\"Leur appréciation doit tenir compte des termes de l’instrument et du contexte économique.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Il faut examiner options, convertibles ou forwards pour vérifier s’ils confèrent un pouvoir actuel et non seulement théorique.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B23\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Determining whether rights are substantive requires judgement, taking into account all facts and circumstances. Factors to consider in making that determination include but are not limited to: (a)Whether there are any barriers (economic or otherwise) that prevent the holder (or holders) from exercising the rights. Examples of such barriers include but are not limited to: (i)financial penalties and incentives that would prevent (or deter) the holder from exercising its rights. (ii)an exercise or conversion price that creates a financial barrier that would prevent (or deter) the holder from exercising its rights. (iii)terms and conditions that make it unlikely that the rights would be exercised, for example, conditions that narrowly limit the timing of their exercise. (iv)the absence of an explicit, reasonable mechanism in the founding documents of an investee or in applicable laws or regulations that would allow the holder to exercise its rights. (v)the inability of the holder of the rights to obtain the information necessary to exercise its rights. (vi)operational barriers or incentives that would prevent (or deter) the holder from exercising its rights (eg the absence of other managers willing or able to provide specialised services or provide the services and take on other interests held by the incumbent manager). (vii)legal or regulatory requirements that prevent the holder from exercising its rights (eg where a foreign investor is prohibited from exercising its rights). (b)When the exercise of rights requires the agreement of more than one party, or when the rights are held by more than one party, whether a mechanism is in place that provides those parties with the practical ability to exercise their rights collectively if they choose to do so. The lack of such a mechanism is an indicator that the rights may not be substantive. The more parties that are required to agree to exercise the rights, the less likely it is that those rights are substantive. However, a board of directors whose members are independent of the decision maker may serve as a mechanism for numerous investors to act collectively in exercising their rights. Therefore, removal rights exercisable by an independent board of directors are more likely to be substantive than if the same rights were exercisable individually by a large number of investors. (c)Whether the party or parties that hold the rights would benefit from the exercise of those rights. For example, the holder of potential voting rights in an investee (see paragraphs B47⁠–⁠B50) shall consider the exercise or conversion price of the instrument. **The terms and conditions of potential voting rights are more likely to be substantive when the instrument is in the money or the investor would benefit for other reasons** (eg by realising synergies between the investor and the investee) from the exercise or conversion of the instrument.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B47\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"When assessing control, an investor considers **its potential voting rights as well as potential voting rights held by other parties**, to determine whether it has power. Potential voting rights are rights to obtain voting rights of an investee, such as those arising from convertible instruments or options, including forward contracts. **Those potential voting rights are considered only if the rights are substantive** (see paragraphs B22⁠–⁠B25).\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B49\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If the investor also has voting or other decision‑making rights relating to the investee’s activities, **the investor assesses whether those rights, in combination with potential voting rights, give the investor power**.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B50\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"**Substantive potential voting rights alone, or in combination with other rights, can give an investor the current ability to direct the relevant activities.** For example, this is likely to be the case when an investor holds 40 per cent of the voting rights of an investee and, in accordance with paragraph B23, holds substantive rights arising from options to acquire a further 20 per cent of the voting rights.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_3\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"contractual_decision_making_power\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Pouvoir par droits contractuels de décision\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Cette approche s’applique à la question, car IFRS 10 précise que le pouvoir peut résulter d’un ou plusieurs accords contractuels lorsque les droits de vote ne sont pas le facteur dominant (IFRS 10 11, B8, B17). Dans cette situation générale, des droits contractuels peuvent établir le pouvoir s’ils donnent la capacité actuelle de diriger les activités pertinentes, y compris au moyen de droits de nomination, de révocation ou de veto sur des transactions significatives (IFRS 10 10, B15, B40, B52, B53).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Les droits contractuels doivent porter sur les activités pertinentes.\\\",\\n        \\\"Ils doivent être actuels et substantifs.\\\",\\n        \\\"Les droits ne doivent pas être uniquement protecteurs.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Il faut identifier les activités pertinentes et lire les contrats pour voir qui peut réellement décider de celles qui affectent le plus les rendements.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"10\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"An investor has power over an investee when the investor has **existing rights that give it the current ability to direct the relevant activities**, ie the activities that significantly affect the investee’s returns. E4\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"11\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Power arises from rights. Sometimes assessing power is straightforward, such as when power over an investee is obtained directly and solely from the voting rights granted by equity instruments such as shares, and can be assessed by considering the voting rights from those shareholdings. **In other cases, the assessment will be more complex and require more than one factor to be considered, for example when power results from one or more contractual arrangements.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B8\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"An investee may be designed so that voting rights are not the dominant factor in deciding who controls the investee, such as when any voting rights relate to administrative tasks only and **the relevant activities are directed by means of contractual arrangements**. In such cases, an investor’s consideration of the purpose and design of the investee shall also include consideration of the risks to which the investee was designed to be exposed, the risks it was designed to pass on to the parties involved with the investee and whether the investor is exposed to some or all of those risks. Consideration of the risks includes not only the downside risk, but also the potential for upside.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B15\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Examples of rights that, either individually or in combination, can give an investor power include but are not limited to: (a)rights in the form of voting rights (or potential voting rights) of an investee (see paragraphs B34⁠–⁠B50); **(b)rights to appoint, reassign or remove members of an investee’s key management personnel who have the ability to direct the relevant activities ; (c)rights to appoint or remove another entity that directs the relevant activities; (d)rights to direct the investee to enter into, or veto any changes to, transactions for the benefit of the investor; (e)other rights (such as decision‑making rights specified in a management contract) that give the holder the ability to direct the relevant activities.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B17\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"When voting rights cannot have a significant effect on an investee’s returns, such as when voting rights relate to administrative tasks only and **contractual arrangements determine the direction of the relevant activities**, the investor needs to assess those contractual arrangements in order to determine whether it has rights sufficient to give it power over the investee. To determine whether an investor has rights sufficient to give it power, the investor shall consider the purpose and design of the investee (see paragraphs B5⁠–⁠B8) and the requirements in paragraphs B51⁠–⁠B54 together with paragraphs B18⁠–⁠B20.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B40\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"**Other decision‑making rights, in combination with voting rights, can give an investor the current ability to direct the relevant activities.** For example, the rights specified in a contractual arrangement in combination with voting rights may be sufficient to give an investor the current ability to direct the manufacturing processes of an investee or to direct other operating or financing activities of an investee that significantly affect the investee’s returns. However, in the absence of any other rights, economic dependence of an investee on the investor (such as relations of a supplier with its main customer) does not lead to the investor having power over the investee.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B52\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In addition, an investor shall consider contractual arrangements such as call rights, put rights and liquidation rights established at the investee’s inception. **When these contractual arrangements involve activities that are closely related to the investee, then these activities are, in substance, an integral part of the investee’s overall activities**, even though they may occur outside the legal boundaries of the investee. Therefore, explicit or implicit decision‑making rights embedded in contractual arrangements that are closely related to the investee need to be considered as relevant activities when determining power over the investee.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B53\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"For some investees, relevant activities occur only when particular circumstances arise or events occur. The investee may be designed so that the direction of its activities and its returns are predetermined unless and until those particular circumstances arise or events occur. In this case, **only the decisions about the investee’s activities when those circumstances or events occur can significantly affect its returns and thus be relevant activities**. The circumstances or events need not have occurred for an investor with the ability to make those decisions to have power. **The fact that the right to make decisions is contingent on circumstances arising or an event occurring does not, in itself, make those rights protective.**\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_4\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"de_facto_power\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Pouvoir de fait\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Cette approche s’applique car IFRS 10 admet que, lorsque l’analyse des droits n’est pas concluante à elle seule, l’investisseur doit apprécier s’il a la capacité pratique de diriger unilatéralement les activités pertinentes (IFRS 10 B18, B41, B45). Dans la situation générale, un pouvoir de fait peut être établi notamment au regard de la dispersion des autres porteurs, des habitudes de vote et d’indices montrant que l’investisseur a plus qu’un intérêt passif (IFRS 10 B19, B20, B43, B45).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"L’investisseur doit avoir la capacité pratique de diriger unilatéralement les activités pertinentes.\\\",\\n        \\\"Les faits et circonstances doivent étayer cette capacité au-delà des seuls droits formels.\\\",\\n        \\\"L’analyse des autres porteurs et de leur comportement doit être cohérente avec une domination effective.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Il faut documenter la dispersion de l’actionnariat, les schémas de vote passés et les relations particulières avec l’entité.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B18\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In some circumstances it may be difficult to determine whether an investor’s rights are sufficient to give it power over an investee. In such cases, to enable the assessment of power to be made, **the investor shall consider evidence of whether it has the practical ability to direct the relevant activities unilaterally**. Consideration is given, but is not limited, to the following, which, when considered together with its rights and the indicators in paragraphs B19 and B20, may provide evidence that the investor’s rights are sufficient to give it power over the investee: (a)The investor can, without having the contractual right to do so, appoint or approve the investee’s key management personnel who have the ability to direct the relevant activities. (b)The investor can, without having the contractual right to do so, direct the investee to enter into, or can veto any changes to, significant transactions for the benefit of the investor. (c)The investor can dominate either the nominations process for electing members of the investee’s governing body or the obtaining of proxies from other holders of voting rights. (d)The investee’s key management personnel are related parties of the investor (for example, the chief executive officer of the investee and the chief executive officer of the investor are the same person). (e)The majority of the members of the investee’s governing body are related parties of the investor.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B19\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Sometimes there will be indications that the investor has a special relationship with the investee, which suggests that the investor has more than a passive interest in the investee. The existence of any individual indicator, or a particular combination of indicators, does not necessarily mean that the power criterion is met. However, having more than a passive interest in the investee may indicate that the investor has other related rights sufficient to give it power or provide evidence of existing power over an investee. For example, the following suggests that **the investor has more than a passive interest in the investee and, in combination with other rights, may indicate power**: (a)The investee’s key management personnel who have the ability to direct the relevant activities are current or previous employees of the investor. (b)The investee’s operations are dependent on the investor, such as in the following situations: (i)The investee depends on the investor to fund a significant portion of its operations. (ii)The investor guarantees a significant portion of the investee’s obligations. (iii)The investee depends on the investor for critical services, technology, supplies or raw materials. (iv)The investor controls assets such as licences or trademarks that are critical to the investee’s operations. (v)The investee depends on the investor for key management personnel, such as when the investor’s personnel have specialised knowledge of the investee’s operations. (c)A significant portion of the investee’s activities either involve or are conducted on behalf of the investor. (d)The investor’s exposure, or rights, to returns from its involvement with the investee is disproportionately greater than its voting or other similar rights. For example, there may be a situation in which an investor is entitled, or exposed, to more than half of the returns of the investee but holds less than half of the voting rights of the investee.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B20\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"The greater an investor’s exposure, or rights, to variability of returns from its involvement with an investee, the greater is the incentive for the investor to obtain rights sufficient to give it power. Therefore, **having a large exposure to variability of returns is an indicator that the investor may have power**. However, the extent of the investor’s exposure does not, in itself, determine whether an investor has power over the investee.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B41\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"An investor with less than a majority of the voting rights has rights that are sufficient to give it power when the investor has **the practical ability to direct the relevant activities unilaterally**.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B43\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"When the direction of relevant activities is determined by majority vote and an investor holds significantly more voting rights than any other vote holder or organised group of vote holders, and the other shareholdings are widely dispersed, it may be clear, after considering the factors listed in paragraph B42(a)⁠–⁠(c) alone, that **the investor has power over the investee**.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B45\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"However, the factors listed in paragraph B42(a)⁠–⁠(c) alone may not be conclusive. If an investor, having considered those factors, is unclear whether it has power, it shall consider **additional facts and circumstances, such as whether other shareholders are passive in nature as demonstrated by voting patterns at previous shareholders’ meetings**. This includes the assessment of the factors set out in paragraph B18 and the indicators in paragraphs B19 and B20. The fewer voting rights the investor holds, and the fewer parties that would need to act together to outvote the investor, the more reliance would be placed on the additional facts and circumstances to assess whether the investor’s rights are sufficient to give it power. When the facts and circumstances in paragraphs B18⁠–⁠B20 are considered together with the investor’s rights, greater weight shall be given to the evidence of power in paragraph B18 than to the indicators of power in paragraphs B19 and B20.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_5\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"principal_decision_maker_power\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Pouvoir du décideur agissant comme principal\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Cette approche s’applique car IFRS 10 exige qu’un décideur détenteur de droits délégués détermine s’il agit comme principal ou comme agent (IFRS 10 B58, B59, B60). Dans la situation générale, les droits délégués permettent d’établir le pouvoir seulement si le décideur agit pour son propre compte comme principal; s’il peut être révoqué sans cause par une seule partie, il est agent et ne contrôle pas l’entité (IFRS 10 B61, B65).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Le décideur doit disposer de droits de décision sur les activités pertinentes.\\\",\\n        \\\"Il doit agir comme principal et non comme agent.\\\",\\n        \\\"Les droits détenus par d’autres parties ne doivent pas démontrer qu’il agit principalement pour leur compte.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Il faut analyser l’étendue du mandat, les droits de révocation, la rémunération et l’exposition du décideur aux rendements variables.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B58\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"When an investor with decision‑making rights (a decision maker) assesses whether it controls an investee, it shall determine **whether it is a principal or an agent**. An investor shall also determine whether another entity with decision‑making rights is acting as an agent for the investor. **An agent is a party primarily engaged to act on behalf and for the benefit of another party or parties (the principal(s)) and therefore does not control the investee when it exercises its decision‑making authority** (see paragraphs 17 and 18). Thus, sometimes a principal’s power may be held and exercisable by an agent, but on behalf of the principal. A decision maker is not an agent simply because other parties can benefit from the decisions that it makes.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B59\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"An investor may delegate its decision‑making authority to an agent on some specific issues or on all relevant activities. When assessing whether it controls an investee, **the investor shall treat the decision‑making rights delegated to its agent as held by the investor directly**. In situations where there is more than one principal, each of the principals shall assess whether it has power over the investee by considering the requirements in paragraphs B5⁠–⁠B54. Paragraphs B60⁠–⁠B72 provide guidance on determining whether a decision maker is an agent or a principal.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B60\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A decision maker shall consider the overall relationship between itself, the investee being managed and other parties involved with the investee, in particular **all the factors below, in determining whether it is an agent**: (a)the scope of its decision‑making authority over the investee (paragraphs B62 and B63). (b)the rights held by other parties (paragraphs B64⁠–⁠B67). (c)the remuneration to which it is entitled in accordance with the remuneration agreement(s) (paragraphs B68⁠–⁠B70). (d)the decision maker’s exposure to variability of returns from other interests that it holds in the investee (paragraphs B71 and B72). Different weightings shall be applied to each of the factors on the basis of particular facts and circumstances.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B61\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Determining whether a decision maker is an agent requires an evaluation of all the factors listed in paragraph B60 **unless a single party holds substantive rights to remove the decision maker (removal rights) and can remove the decision maker without cause** (see paragraph B65).\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B65\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"When a single party holds substantive removal rights and can **remove the decision maker without cause, this, in isolation, is sufficient to conclude that the decision maker is an agent**. If more than one party holds such rights (and no individual party can remove the decision maker without the agreement of other parties) those rights are not, in isolation, conclusive in determining that a decision maker acts primarily on behalf and for the benefit of others. In addition, the greater the number of parties required to act together to exercise rights to remove a decision maker and the greater the magnitude of, and variability associated with, the decision maker’s other economic interests (ie remuneration and other interests), the less the weighting that shall be placed on this factor.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    }\\n  ]\\n}\"}","expires":1778936059867}

cache: keyv:repeat:0:exec:uv run python /Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/scripts/run_answer.py:4606dc2dc0f5c27588a548eb91f4dfc4f2d83a6b2066151c169a0cb3228e57d0:Une entité est détenue ou influencée par plusieurs parties. Sur quelle base IFRS faut-il conclure qu’un investisseur, plutôt qu’un autre, détient le pouvoir sur cette entité ?:{"id":"exec:uv run python /Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/scripts/run_answer.py","config":{"policy-config":"./effective/policy.default.yaml","llm_provider":"openai-codex","basePath":"/Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/experiments/57_answer-evals_on_variants_for_Q5_Q6_Q9_Q12_Q16/runs/2026-05-02_12-49-24_promptfoo-eval-family-q6"},"env":{}}

cache.value: {"value":"{\"output\":\"{\\n  \\\"assumptions_fr\\\": [\\n    \\\"La question est comprise comme une analyse de principe IFRS visant à déterminer quelle partie, parmi plusieurs investisseurs ou parties impliquées, doit être considérée comme détenant le pouvoir sur l’entité.\\\",\\n    \\\"Aucun fait détaillé n’est fourni sur les droits de vote, accords contractuels, droits de nomination/révocation, droits potentiels, droits protectifs, statut principal/agent ou comportement d’autres parties ; la conclusion est donc formulée comme une base de décision conditionnelle.\\\"\\n  ],\\n  \\\"recommendation\\\": {\\n    \\\"answer\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n    \\\"justification\\\": \\\"La base IFRS à appliquer en premier est IFRS 10 : il faut identifier l’investisseur qui a le pouvoir sur les activités pertinentes, l’exposition à des rendements variables et la capacité d’utiliser ce pouvoir pour affecter ces rendements. Si aucun investisseur ne remplit seul ce test, il faut basculer vers IFRS 11 (contrôle conjoint) ou IAS 28 (influence notable).\\\"\\n  },\\n  \\\"approaches\\\": [\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_1\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"control\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Contrôle\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, la base IFRS prioritaire pour conclure qu’un investisseur, plutôt qu’un autre, détient le pouvoir est IFRS 10 : le contrôlant est celui qui réunit pouvoir, rendements variables et lien entre les deux (IFRS 10 6, IFRS 10 7, IFRS 10 10). Si plusieurs parties ont des droits sur des activités différentes, le pouvoir revient à celle qui dirige l’activité pertinente la plus significative ; il faut aussi tenir compte des droits substantifs, des droits protectifs, des accords contractuels, des droits de vote de fait, des droits potentiels et du statut principal/agent (IFRS 10 13, IFRS 10 B22, IFRS 10 B26, IFRS 10 B42, IFRS 10 B58).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Conclure qu’un investisseur a des droits existants et substantifs lui donnant actuellement la capacité de diriger les activités pertinentes les plus significatives.\\\",\\n        \\\"Établir que cet investisseur est exposé à des rendements variables et peut utiliser son pouvoir pour affecter ces rendements.\\\",\\n        \\\"Vérifier qu’il n’agit pas seulement comme agent d’une autre partie.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si ces conditions sont remplies, cet investisseur contrôle l’entité et les autres modèles ne s’appliquent pas en priorité.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"6\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"An investor controls an investee when **it is exposed, or has rights, to variable returns from its involvement with the investee and has the ability to affect those returns through its power over the investee**.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"7\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Thus, an investor controls an investee if and only if the investor has all the following:\\\\n(a)**power over the investee** (see paragraphs 10⁠–⁠14);\\\\n(b)**exposure, or rights, to variable returns from its involvement with the investee** (see paragraphs 15 and 16) ; and\\\\n(c)**the ability to use its power over the investee to affect the amount of the investor’s returns** (see paragraphs 17 and 18).\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"10\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"An investor has power over an investee when **the investor has existing rights that give it the current ability to direct the relevant activities**, ie **the activities that significantly affect the investee’s returns**. E4\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"13\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If two or more investors each have existing rights that give them the unilateral ability to direct different relevant activities, **the investor that has the current ability to direct the activities that most significantly affect the returns of the investee has power over the investee**.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B22\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"An investor, in assessing whether it has power, considers only **substantive rights** relating to an investee (held by the investor and others). **For a right to be substantive, the holder must have the practical ability to exercise that right**.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B26\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In evaluating whether rights give an investor power over an investee, the investor shall assess whether its rights, and rights held by others, are **protective rights**. Protective rights relate to fundamental changes to the activities of an investee or apply in exceptional circumstances. However, **not all rights that apply in exceptional circumstances or are contingent on events are protective** (see paragraphs B13 and B53). E12\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B42\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"When assessing whether an investor’s voting rights are sufficient to give it power, an investor considers **all facts and circumstances**, including:\\\\n(a)the size of the investor’s holding of voting rights relative to the size and dispersion of holdings of the other vote holders, noting that:\\\\n(i)the more voting rights an investor holds, the more likely the investor is to have existing rights that give it the current ability to direct the relevant activities ;\\\\n(ii)the more voting rights an investor holds relative to other vote holders, the more likely the investor is to have existing rights that give it the current ability to direct the relevant activities;\\\\n(iii)the more parties that would need to act together to outvote the investor, the more likely the investor is to have existing rights that give it the current ability to direct the relevant activities;\\\\n(b)potential voting rights held by the investor, other vote holders or other parties (see paragraphs B47⁠–⁠B50);\\\\n(c)rights arising from other contractual arrangements (see paragraph B40); and\\\\n(d)any additional facts and circumstances that indicate the investor has, or does not have, the current ability to direct the relevant activities at the time that decisions need to be made, including voting patterns at previous shareholders’ meetings.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B58\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"When an investor with decision‑making rights (a decision maker) assesses whether it controls an investee, **it shall determine whether it is a principal or an agent**. An investor shall also determine whether another entity with decision‑making rights is acting as an agent for the investor. **An agent is a party primarily engaged to act on behalf and for the benefit of another party or parties (the principal(s)) and therefore does not control the investee when it exercises its decision‑making authority** (see paragraphs 17 and 18). Thus, sometimes a principal’s power may be held and exercisable by an agent, but on behalf of the principal. A decision maker is not an agent simply because other parties can benefit from the decisions that it makes.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_2\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"joint_control\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Contrôle conjoint\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Cette approche ne s’applique dans cette situation que si aucun investisseur ne peut diriger seul les activités pertinentes et que les parties doivent agir ensemble. IFRS 10 précise que, lorsque les investisseurs doivent coopérer pour diriger les activités pertinentes, aucun ne contrôle individuellement l’entité ; IFRS 11 exige alors que les décisions sur ces activités requièrent l’unanimité des parties partageant le contrôle (IFRS 10 9, IFRS 11 7, IFRS 11 9).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Les parties qui contrôlent l’entité doivent agir ensemble pour diriger les activités qui affectent significativement les rendements.\\\",\\n        \\\"Les décisions relatives aux activités pertinentes doivent requérir le consentement unanime des parties qui contrôlent collectivement l’entité.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si ces conditions sont remplies, il n’y a pas de contrôlant unique ; l’analyse relève du contrôle conjoint et non du contrôle exclusif.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"9\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Two or more investors collectively control an investee when **they must act together to direct the relevant activities**. In such cases, because **no investor can direct the activities without the co-operation of the others, no investor individually controls the investee**. Each investor would account for its interest in the investee in accordance with the relevant IFRSs, such as IFRS 11 Joint Arrangements, IAS 28 Investments in Associates and Joint Ventures or IFRS 9 Financial Instruments.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs11\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"7\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"**Joint control is the contractually agreed sharing of control of an arrangement**, which exists only when **decisions about the relevant activities require the unanimous consent of the parties sharing control**.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs11\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"9\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Once it has been determined that all the parties, or a group of the parties, control the arrangement collectively, **joint control exists only when decisions about the relevant activities require the unanimous consent of the parties that control the arrangement collectively**.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_3\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"significant_influence\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Influence notable\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Cette approche n’est pertinente dans cette situation que si l’investisseur ne contrôle pas seul l’entité et n’a pas non plus de contrôle conjoint, mais conserve un pouvoir de participation aux décisions financières et opérationnelles. IAS 28 donne des indices d’influence notable, notamment une représentation au conseil ou une participation au processus d’élaboration des politiques ; une détention de 20 % ou plus crée une présomption, réfragable, d’influence notable (IAS 28 5, IAS 28 6).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Absence de contrôle au sens d’IFRS 10 et absence de contrôle conjoint au sens d’IFRS 11.\\\",\\n        \\\"Existence d’une capacité à participer aux décisions de politique financière et opérationnelle, par exemple via conseil, processus décisionnels ou autres indices IAS 28.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si ces conditions sont remplies, l’investisseur n’est pas celui qui détient le pouvoir, mais il peut avoir une influence notable sur l’entité.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ias28\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"5\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If an entity holds, directly or indirectly (eg through subsidiaries), **20 per cent or more of the voting power of the investee, it is presumed that the entity has significant influence**, unless it can be clearly demonstrated that this is not the case. Conversely, if the entity holds, directly or indirectly (eg through subsidiaries), less than 20 per cent of the voting power of the investee, it is presumed that the entity does not have significant influence, unless such influence can be clearly demonstrated. A substantial or majority ownership by another investor does not necessarily preclude an entity from having significant influence.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ias28\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"6\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"The existence of significant influence by an entity is usually evidenced in one or more of the following ways:\\\\n(a)**representation on the board of directors or equivalent governing body of the investee**;\\\\n(b)**participation in policy-making processes, including participation in decisions about dividends or other distributions**;\\\\n(c)material transactions between the entity and its investee;\\\\n(d)interchange of managerial personnel; or\\\\n(e)provision of essential technical information.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    }\\n  ]\\n}\"}","expires":1778936150798}

cache: keyv:repeat:1:exec:uv run python /Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/scripts/run_answer.py:4606dc2dc0f5c27588a548eb91f4dfc4f2d83a6b2066151c169a0cb3228e57d0:Dans l’analyse du contrôle, comment apprécier si les droits détenus par un investisseur sont suffisants pour diriger les activités pertinentes de l’entité ?:{"id":"exec:uv run python /Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/scripts/run_answer.py","config":{"policy-config":"./effective/policy.default.yaml","llm_provider":"openai-codex","basePath":"/Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/experiments/57_answer-evals_on_variants_for_Q5_Q6_Q9_Q12_Q16/runs/2026-05-02_12-49-24_promptfoo-eval-family-q6"},"env":{}}

cache.value: {"value":"{\"output\":\"{\\n  \\\"assumptions_fr\\\": [\\n    \\\"La question est posée de manière générale, sans faits spécifiques sur la structure de l’entité, la répartition des droits de vote ou les stipulations contractuelles ; l’analyse décrit donc les critères IFRS 10 à appliquer pour apprécier la suffisance des droits.\\\",\\n    \\\"On suppose que l’objectif est d’évaluer si les droits détenus par un investisseur lui donnent le pouvoir de diriger les activités pertinentes d’une entité au sens d’IFRS 10.\\\"\\n  ],\\n  \\\"recommendation\\\": {\\n    \\\"answer\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n    \\\"justification\\\": \\\"Oui, mais uniquement après avoir identifié les activités pertinentes, la manière dont les décisions sont prises et vérifié que les droits sont substantifs et non protecteurs. Selon les faits, la suffisance des droits peut résulter de droits de vote majoritaires, d’un contrôle de fait, de droits contractuels ou de droits de vote potentiels substantifs.\\\"\\n  },\\n  \\\"approaches\\\": [\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_1\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"majority_voting_rights\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Droits de vote majoritaires\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Cette voie s’applique dans cette situation seulement si les activités pertinentes sont dirigées par vote et si l’investisseur détient plus de la moitié des droits de vote. IFRS 10 indique alors qu’il a en principe le pouvoir, sous réserve que ces droits soient existants, substantifs et qu’aucune exception des paragraphes B36-B37 ne s’applique (IFRS 10 B34-B35, 10, B9).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Les activités pertinentes sont dirigées par des droits de vote ou par la nomination d’un organe décisionnaire via ces droits.\\\",\\n        \\\"L’investisseur détient plus de la moitié des droits de vote.\\\",\\n        \\\"Les droits sont substantifs et non simplement protecteurs.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si ces conditions sont remplies, les droits de vote majoritaires suffisent normalement à établir le pouvoir sur l’entité.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B34\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Often an investor has the current ability, through voting or similar rights, to direct the relevant activities. **An investor considers the requirements in this section (paragraphs B35⁠–⁠B50) if the relevant activities of an investee are directed through voting rights.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B35\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"**An investor that holds more than half of the voting rights of an investee has power** in the following situations, unless paragraph B36 or paragraph B37 applies:\\\\n(a)the relevant activities are directed by a vote of the holder of the majority of the voting rights, or\\\\n(b)a majority of the members of the governing body that directs the relevant activities are appointed by a vote of the holder of the majority of the voting rights.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"10\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"**An investor has power over an investee when the investor has existing rights that give it the current ability to direct the relevant activities**, ie the activities that significantly affect the investee’s returns. E4\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B9\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"To have power over an investee, an investor must have existing rights that give it the current ability to direct the relevant activities. **For the purpose of assessing power, only substantive rights and rights that are not protective shall be considered** (see paragraphs B22⁠–⁠B28).\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_2\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"de_facto_control_through_voting_rights\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Contrôle de fait par droits de vote\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Cette voie s’applique dans cette situation si l’investisseur détient moins de 50 % des droits de vote mais conserve, en pratique, la capacité unilatérale de diriger les activités pertinentes. IFRS 10 impose alors d’examiner la taille relative de la participation, la dispersion des autres porteurs, les autres droits et les schémas de vote passés ; à défaut de conclusion claire, il n’y a pas contrôle (IFRS 10 B38, B41-B46).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"L’investisseur détient moins de la majorité des droits de vote.\\\",\\n        \\\"Les autres porteurs sont suffisamment dispersés ou passifs pour laisser à l’investisseur une capacité pratique unilatérale.\\\",\\n        \\\"Les autres faits et circonstances n’indiquent pas qu’un petit nombre d’autres investisseurs peut bloquer cette direction.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Une participation minoritaire peut suffire, mais seulement si les faits démontrent une capacité pratique de direction unilatérale.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B38\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"**An investor can have power even if it holds less than a majority of the voting rights of an investee.** An investor can have power with less than a majority of the voting rights of an investee, for example, through:\\\\n(a)a contractual arrangement between the investor and other vote holders (see paragraph B39);\\\\n(b)rights arising from other contractual arrangements (see paragraph B40);\\\\n(c)the investor’s voting rights (see paragraphs B41⁠–⁠B45);\\\\n(d)potential voting rights (see paragraphs B47⁠–⁠B50); or\\\\n(e)a combination of (a)⁠–⁠(d).\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B41\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"**An investor with less than a majority of the voting rights has rights that are sufficient to give it power when the investor has the practical ability to direct the relevant activities unilaterally.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B42\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"When assessing whether an investor’s voting rights are sufficient to give it power, an investor considers **all facts and circumstances**, including:\\\\n(a)the size of the investor’s holding of voting rights relative to the size and dispersion of holdings of the other vote holders, noting that:\\\\n(i)the more voting rights an investor holds, the more likely the investor is to have existing rights that give it the current ability to direct the relevant activities ;\\\\n(ii)the more voting rights an investor holds relative to other vote holders, the more likely the investor is to have existing rights that give it the current ability to direct the relevant activities;\\\\n(iii)the more parties that would need to act together to outvote the investor, the more likely the investor is to have existing rights that give it the current ability to direct the relevant activities;\\\\n(b)potential voting rights held by the investor, other vote holders or other parties (see paragraphs B47⁠–⁠B50);\\\\n(c)rights arising from other contractual arrangements (see paragraph B40); and\\\\n(d)any additional facts and circumstances that indicate the investor has, or does not have, the current ability to direct the relevant activities at the time that decisions need to be made, including voting patterns at previous shareholders’ meetings.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B45\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"However, the factors listed in paragraph B42(a)⁠–⁠(c) alone may not be conclusive. **If an investor, having considered those factors, is unclear whether it has power, it shall consider additional facts and circumstances**, such as whether other shareholders are passive in nature as demonstrated by voting patterns at previous shareholders’ meetings. This includes the assessment of the factors set out in paragraph B18 and the indicators in paragraphs B19 and B20. The fewer voting rights the investor holds, and the fewer parties that would need to act together to outvote the investor, the more reliance would be placed on the additional facts and circumstances to assess whether the investor’s rights are sufficient to give it power. When the facts and circumstances in paragraphs B18⁠–⁠B20 are considered together with the investor’s rights, greater weight shall be given to the evidence of power in paragraph B18 than to the indicators of power in paragraphs B19 and B20.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B46\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"**If it is not clear, having considered the factors listed in paragraph B42(a)⁠–⁠(d), that the investor has power, the investor does not control the investee.**\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_3\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"contractual_power_over_relevant_activities\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Pouvoir contractuel sur les activités pertinentes\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Cette voie s’applique dans cette situation lorsque les droits de vote ne sont pas le facteur déterminant et que le pouvoir découle d’accords contractuels ou d’autres droits décisionnels. IFRS 10 demande alors d’identifier l’objet et la conception de l’entité, les activités pertinentes et de vérifier si les droits donnent la capacité actuelle de les diriger ; seuls les droits substantifs, non protecteurs, sont pris en compte (IFRS 10 11, B5, B8, B15, B17, B22, B26-B27).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Les droits de vote ne déterminent pas, à eux seuls, la direction des activités pertinentes.\\\",\\n        \\\"Des accords contractuels ou autres droits décisionnels portent sur les activités qui affectent significativement les rendements.\\\",\\n        \\\"Ces droits sont substantifs et ne sont pas uniquement protecteurs.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Le contrôle peut être établi sans majorité de vote si les contrats donnent la capacité actuelle de diriger les activités pertinentes.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"11\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Power arises from rights. Sometimes assessing power is straightforward, such as when power over an investee is obtained directly and solely from the voting rights granted by equity instruments such as shares, and can be assessed by considering the voting rights from those shareholdings. **In other cases, the assessment will be more complex and require more than one factor to be considered, for example when power results from one or more contractual arrangements.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B5\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"**When assessing control of an investee, an investor shall consider the purpose and design of the investee** in order to identify the relevant activities, how decisions about the relevant activities are made, who has the current ability to direct those activities and who receives returns from those activities.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B8\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"**An investee may be designed so that voting rights are not the dominant factor in deciding who controls the investee**, such as when any voting rights relate to administrative tasks only and the relevant activities are directed by means of contractual arrangements. In such cases, an investor’s consideration of the purpose and design of the investee shall also include consideration of the risks to which the investee was designed to be exposed, the risks it was designed to pass on to the parties involved with the investee and whether the investor is exposed to some or all of those risks. Consideration of the risks includes not only the downside risk, but also the potential for upside.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B15\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Examples of rights that, either individually or in combination, can give an investor power include but are not limited to:\\\\n(a)rights in the form of voting rights (or potential voting rights) of an investee (see paragraphs B34⁠–⁠B50);\\\\n(b)**rights to appoint, reassign or remove members of an investee’s key management personnel who have the ability to direct the relevant activities** ;\\\\n(c)**rights to appoint or remove another entity that directs the relevant activities**;\\\\n(d)**rights to direct the investee to enter into, or veto any changes to, transactions for the benefit of the investor**; and\\\\n(e)**other rights (such as decision‑making rights specified in a management contract) that give the holder the ability to direct the relevant activities.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B17\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"**When voting rights cannot have a significant effect on an investee’s returns**, such as when voting rights relate to administrative tasks only and contractual arrangements determine the direction of the relevant activities, the investor needs to assess those contractual arrangements in order to determine whether it has rights sufficient to give it power over the investee. To determine whether an investor has rights sufficient to give it power, the investor shall consider the purpose and design of the investee (see paragraphs B5⁠–⁠B8) and the requirements in paragraphs B51⁠–⁠B54 together with paragraphs B18⁠–⁠B20.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B22\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"An investor, in assessing whether it has power, considers only substantive rights relating to an investee (held by the investor and others). **For a right to be substantive, the holder must have the practical ability to exercise that right.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B26\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In evaluating whether rights give an investor power over an investee, the investor shall assess whether its rights, and rights held by others, are protective rights. **Protective rights relate to fundamental changes to the activities of an investee or apply in exceptional circumstances.** However, not all rights that apply in exceptional circumstances or are contingent on events are protective (see paragraphs B13 and B53). E12\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B27\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Because protective rights are designed to protect the interests of their holder without giving that party power over the investee to which those rights relate, **an investor that holds only protective rights cannot have power or prevent another party from having power over an investee** (see paragraph 14).\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_4\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"substantive_potential_voting_rights\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Droits de vote potentiels substantifs\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Cette voie s’applique dans cette situation si l’investisseur détient des options, instruments convertibles ou contrats à terme qui lui donnent, seuls ou avec d’autres droits, la capacité actuelle de diriger les activités pertinentes. IFRS 10 précise que ces droits potentiels ne comptent que s’ils sont substantifs, ce qui suppose une appréciation de leurs termes, de leur exercice pratique et de la conception de l’instrument (IFRS 10 B47-B50, B23-B24).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"L’investisseur détient des droits potentiels d’obtenir des droits de vote.\\\",\\n        \\\"Ces droits potentiels sont substantifs au moment où les décisions doivent être prises.\\\",\\n        \\\"Pris seuls ou avec d’autres droits, ils donnent la capacité actuelle de diriger les activités pertinentes.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Des droits potentiels substantifs peuvent suffire à conférer le pouvoir avant même leur exercice effectif.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B47\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"When assessing control, an investor considers its potential voting rights as well as potential voting rights held by other parties, to determine whether it has power. Potential voting rights are rights to obtain voting rights of an investee, such as those arising from convertible instruments or options, including forward contracts. **Those potential voting rights are considered only if the rights are substantive** (see paragraphs B22⁠–⁠B25).\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B48\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"When considering potential voting rights, an investor shall consider **the purpose and design of the instrument**, as well as the purpose and design of any other involvement the investor has with the investee. This includes an assessment of the various terms and conditions of the instrument as well as the investor’s apparent expectations, motives and reasons for agreeing to those terms and conditions.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B49\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If the investor also has voting or other decision‑making rights relating to the investee’s activities, the investor assesses whether **those rights, in combination with potential voting rights, give the investor power.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B50\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"**Substantive potential voting rights alone, or in combination with other rights, can give an investor the current ability to direct the relevant activities.** For example, this is likely to be the case when an investor holds 40 per cent of the voting rights of an investee and, in accordance with paragraph B23, holds substantive rights arising from options to acquire a further 20 per cent of the voting rights.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B23\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Determining whether rights are substantive requires judgement, taking into account all facts and circumstances. Factors to consider in making that determination include but are not limited to:\\\\n(a)Whether there are any barriers (economic or otherwise) that prevent the holder (or holders) from exercising the rights. Examples of such barriers include but are not limited to:\\\\n(i)financial penalties and incentives that would prevent (or deter) the holder from exercising its rights.\\\\n(ii)an exercise or conversion price that creates a financial barrier that would prevent (or deter) the holder from exercising its rights.\\\\n(iii)terms and conditions that make it unlikely that the rights would be exercised, for example, conditions that narrowly limit the timing of their exercise.\\\\n(iv)the absence of an explicit, reasonable mechanism in the founding documents of an investee or in applicable laws or regulations that would allow the holder to exercise its rights.\\\\n(v)the inability of the holder of the rights to obtain the information necessary to exercise its rights.\\\\n(vi)operational barriers or incentives that would prevent (or deter) the holder from exercising its rights (eg the absence of other managers willing or able to provide specialised services or provide the services and take on other interests held by the incumbent manager).\\\\n(vii)legal or regulatory requirements that prevent the holder from exercising its rights (eg where a foreign investor is prohibited from exercising its rights).\\\\n(b)When the exercise of rights requires the agreement of more than one party, or when the rights are held by more than one party, whether a mechanism is in place that provides those parties with the practical ability to exercise their rights collectively if they choose to do so. The lack of such a mechanism is an indicator that the rights may not be substantive. The more parties that are required to agree to exercise the rights, the less likely it is that those rights are substantive. However, a board of directors whose members are independent of the decision maker may serve as a mechanism for numerous investors to act collectively in exercising their rights. Therefore, removal rights exercisable by an independent board of directors are more likely to be substantive than if the same rights were exercisable individually by a large number of investors.\\\\n(c)Whether the party or parties that hold the rights would benefit from the exercise of those rights. For example, the holder of potential voting rights in an investee (see paragraphs B47⁠–⁠B50) shall consider the exercise or conversion price of the instrument. **The terms and conditions of potential voting rights are more likely to be substantive when the instrument is in the money or the investor would benefit for other reasons** (eg by realising synergies between the investor and the investee) from the exercise or conversion of the instrument.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B24\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"To be substantive, rights also need to be exercisable **when decisions about the direction of the relevant activities need to be made**. Usually, to be substantive, the rights need to be currently exercisable. However, sometimes rights can be substantive, even though the rights are not currently exercisable.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    }\\n  ]\\n}\"}","expires":1778936166843}

cache: keyv:repeat:0:exec:uv run python /Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/scripts/run_answer.py:4606dc2dc0f5c27588a548eb91f4dfc4f2d83a6b2066151c169a0cb3228e57d0:Dans l’analyse du contrôle, comment apprécier si les droits détenus par un investisseur sont suffisants pour diriger les activités pertinentes de l’entité ?:{"id":"exec:uv run python /Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/scripts/run_answer.py","config":{"policy-config":"./effective/policy.default.yaml","llm_provider":"openai-codex","basePath":"/Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/experiments/57_answer-evals_on_variants_for_Q5_Q6_Q9_Q12_Q16/runs/2026-05-02_12-49-24_promptfoo-eval-family-q6"},"env":{}}

cache.value: {"value":"{\"output\":\"{\\n  \\\"assumptions_fr\\\": [\\n    \\\"La question est traitée de manière générale, sans jeu de faits spécifique sur la répartition des droits de vote, les pactes, les options ou les droits de retrait.\\\",\\n    \\\"On suppose que l’objectif est d’expliquer, selon IFRS 10, la méthode d’appréciation du caractère suffisant des droits pour diriger les activités pertinentes d’une entité.\\\"\\n  ],\\n  \\\"recommendation\\\": {\\n    \\\"answer\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n    \\\"justification\\\": \\\"Selon IFRS 10, l’appréciation repose d’abord sur l’identification des activités pertinentes et des droits substantifs donnant la capacité actuelle de les diriger. Le traitement applicable dépend ensuite des faits: majorité des droits de vote, pouvoir de fait, droits contractuels, droits potentiels, caractère protecteur des droits, et éventuellement analyse principal/agent.\\\"\\n  },\\n  \\\"approaches\\\": [\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_1\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"substantive_majority_voting_rights\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Majorité de droits de vote substantifs\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Cette approche s’applique si, dans la situation analysée, les activités pertinentes sont dirigées par vote et que l’investisseur détient plus de la moitié des droits de vote avec des droits substantifs. IFRS 10 précise qu’un investisseur a le pouvoir lorsqu’il a des droits existants donnant la capacité actuelle de diriger les activités pertinentes (IFRS 10 10) et que la détention de plus de la moitié des droits de vote confère en principe ce pouvoir, sauf droits concurrents d’un autre tiers (IFRS 10 B35-B36).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"Les activités pertinentes sont dirigées par vote ou par nomination d’un organe dirigeant\\\",\\n        \\\"Les droits de vote de l’investisseur sont substantifs\\\",\\n        \\\"Aucun autre tiers ne détient des droits existants lui donnant la capacité actuelle de diriger les activités pertinentes\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Si ces conditions sont remplies, les droits sont en principe suffisants pour établir le pouvoir au sens d’IFRS 10.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs10\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"10\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"An investor has power over an investee when the investor has **existing rights that give it the current ability to direct the relevant activities**, ie the activities that significantly affect the investee’s returns.
