cache.value: {"value":"{\"output\":\"{\\n  \\\"assumptions_fr\\\": [\\n    \\\"On suppose que le contrat relève d’IFRS 15 et porte sur une licence de propriété intellectuelle accordée à un client.\\\",\\n    \\\"On suppose que la licence est analysée comme une promesse potentielle de performance et qu’il faut déterminer si elle est distincte des autres biens ou services promis.\\\",\\n    \\\"Faute de faits supplémentaires, il n’est pas possible de trancher définitivement entre un droit d’accès et un droit d’utilisation pour cette licence précise.\\\"\\n  ],\\n  \\\"recommendation\\\": {\\n    \\\"answer\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n    \\\"justification\\\": \\\"Sous IFRS 15, le revenu d’une licence de PI peut être reconnu soit progressivement, soit à un moment précis. Cela dépend d’abord du caractère distinct de la licence, puis de la qualification en droit d’accès ou en droit d’utilisation (IFRS 15 B54–B56, B58, B60–B61).\\\"\\n  },\\n  \\\"approaches\\\": [\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_1\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"right_to_access_intellectual_property\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Droit d’accès à la propriété intellectuelle\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Cette approche s’applique dans cette situation seulement si la licence est distincte des autres promesses du contrat; sinon elle est combinée avec celles-ci et l’analyse de timing se fait au niveau de l’obligation de prestation globale (IFRS 15 B53–B55, 27, 30). Si la licence est distincte et que les trois critères de droit d’accès sont remplis, le revenu est reconnu progressivement sur la période de licence, car l’obligation est satisfaite au fil du temps (IFRS 15 B58, B60, 35(a)).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"La licence est distincte des autres biens ou services promis dans le contrat.\\\",\\n        \\\"Le contrat impose, ou le client s’attend raisonnablement à, des activités de l’entité qui affectent significativement la propriété intellectuelle.\\\",\\n        \\\"Les droits concédés exposent directement le client aux effets positifs ou négatifs de ces activités, sans transfert séparé de bien ou service au fur et à mesure.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Le chiffre d’affaires est étalé sur la période pendant laquelle le client reçoit l’accès continu à la propriété intellectuelle.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs15\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B53\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In addition to a promise to grant a licence (or licences) to a customer, an entity may also promise to transfer other goods or services to the customer. Those promises may be explicitly stated in the contract or implied by an entity’s customary business practices, published policies or specific statements (see paragraph 24). As with other types of contracts, when a contract with a customer includes a promise to grant a licence (or licences) in addition to other promised goods or services, **an entity applies paragraphs 22⁠–⁠30 to identify each of the performance obligations in the contract.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs15\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B54\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If the promise to grant the licence is **not distinct from other promised goods or services in the contract** in accordance with paragraphs 26⁠–⁠30, an entity shall account for the promise to grant a licence and those other promised goods or services together as a single performance obligation. Examples of licences that are not distinct from other goods or services promised in the contract include the following:\\\\n(a)a licence that forms a component of a tangible good and that is integral to the functionality of the good; and\\\\n(b)a licence that the customer can benefit from only in conjunction with a related service (such as an online service provided by the entity that enables, by granting a licence, the customer to access content).\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs15\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B55\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If the licence is not distinct, an entity shall apply paragraphs 31⁠–⁠38 to determine whether the performance obligation (which includes the promised licence) is a performance obligation that is **satisfied over time or satisfied at a point in time.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs15\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"27\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A good or service that is promised to a customer is distinct E3 if both of the following criteria are met:\\\\n(a)the customer can benefit from the good or service either on its own or together with other resources that are readily available to the customer (ie **the good or service is capable of being distinct**); and\\\\n(b)the entity’s promise to transfer the good or service to the customer is **separately identifiable from other promises in the contract** (ie the promise to transfer the good or service is distinct within the context of the contract).\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs15\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"30\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If a promised good or service is **not distinct**, an entity shall combine that good or service with other promised goods or services until it identifies a bundle of goods or services that is distinct. In some cases, that would result in the entity accounting for **all the goods or services promised in a contract as a single performance obligation.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs15\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B58\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"The nature of an entity’s promise in granting a licence is a promise to provide a **right to access the entity’s intellectual property** if all of the following criteria are met:\\\\n(a)the contract requires, or the customer reasonably expects, that the entity will undertake activities that **significantly affect the intellectual property** to which the customer has rights (see paragraphs B59 and B59A);\\\\n(b)the rights granted by the licence **directly expose the customer** to any positive or negative effects of the entity’s activities identified in paragraph B58(a) ; and\\\\n(c)those activities **do not result in the transfer of a good or a service** to the customer as those activities occur (see paragraph 25).\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs15\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B60\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If the criteria in paragraph B58 are met, an entity shall account for the promise to grant a licence as a performance obligation **satisfied over time** because the customer will **simultaneously receive and consume the benefit** from the entity’s performance of providing access to its intellectual property as the performance occurs (see paragraph 35(a)). An entity shall apply paragraphs 39⁠–⁠45 to select an appropriate method to measure its progress towards complete satisfaction of that performance obligation to provide access.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs15\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"35\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"An entity transfers control of a good or service **over time** and, therefore, satisfies a performance obligation and recognises revenue over time, if one of the following criteria is met: E4, E5\\\\n(a)**the customer simultaneously receives and consumes the benefits provided by the entity’s performance as the entity performs** (see paragraphs B3⁠–⁠B4);\\\\n(b)the entity’s performance creates or enhances an asset (for example, work in progress) that the customer controls as the asset is created or enhanced (see paragraph B5); or\\\\n(c)the entity’s performance does not create an asset with an alternative use to the entity (see paragraph 36) and the entity has an enforceable right to payment for performance completed to date (see paragraph 37). E6\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_2\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"right_to_use_intellectual_property\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Droit d’utilisation de la propriété intellectuelle\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Cette approche s’applique dans cette situation si la licence est distincte et si les critères du droit d’accès ne sont pas remplis; dans ce cas, la nature de la promesse est un droit d’utiliser la PI telle qu’elle existe à la date d’octroi (IFRS 15 B56, B61). Le revenu est alors reconnu à un moment précis, à la date de transfert du contrôle de la licence, sans pouvoir être reconnu avant le début de la période d’utilisation effective par le client (IFRS 15 B61, 38).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"La licence est distincte des autres biens ou services promis dans le contrat.\\\",\\n        \\\"Les critères du droit d’accès prévus par IFRS 15 B58 ne sont pas remplis.\\\",\\n        \\\"La licence a été transférée au client et la période pendant laquelle il peut l’utiliser et en tirer bénéfice a commencé.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Le chiffre d’affaires est comptabilisé en une fois lorsque le contrôle de la licence est transféré au client.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs15\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B56\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If the promise to grant the licence is **distinct from the other promised goods or services in the contract** and, therefore, the promise to grant the licence is a separate performance obligation, an entity shall determine whether the licence transfers to a customer either **at a point in time or over time**. In making this determination, an entity shall consider whether the nature of the entity’s promise in granting the licence to a customer is to provide the customer with either:\\\\n(a)a right to access the entity’s intellectual property as it exists throughout the licence period; or\\\\n(b)**a right to use the entity’s intellectual property as it exists at the point in time at which the licence is granted.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs15\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B61\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If the criteria in paragraph B58 are **not met**, the nature of an entity’s promise is to provide a **right to use the entity’s intellectual property** as that intellectual property exists (in terms of form and functionality) at the point in time at which the licence is granted to the customer. This means that the customer can direct the use of, and obtain substantially all of the remaining benefits from, the licence **at the point in time at which the licence transfers**. An entity shall account for the promise to provide a right to use the entity’s intellectual property as a performance obligation **satisfied at a point in time**. An entity shall apply paragraph 38 to determine the point in time at which the licence transfers to the customer. However, **revenue cannot be recognised** for a licence that provides a right to use the entity’s intellectual property **before the beginning of the period during which the customer is able to use and benefit from the licence**. For example, if a software licence period begins before an entity provides (or otherwise makes available) to the customer a code that enables the customer to immediately use the software, the entity would not recognise revenue before that code has been provided (or otherwise made available).\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs15\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"38\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If a performance obligation is **not satisfied over time** in accordance with paragraphs 35⁠–⁠37, an entity satisfies the performance obligation **at a point in time**. To determine the point in time at which a customer obtains control of a promised asset and the entity satisfies a performance obligation, the entity shall consider the requirements for control in paragraphs 31⁠–⁠34. In addition, an entity shall consider indicators of the transfer of control, which include, but are not limited to, the following:\\\\n(a)The entity has a present right to payment for the asset—if a customer is presently obliged to pay for an asset, then that may indicate that the customer has obtained the ability to direct the use of, and obtain substantially all of the remaining benefits from, the asset in exchange.\\\\n(b)The customer has legal title to the asset—legal title may indicate which party to a contract has the ability to direct the use of, and obtain substantially all of the remaining benefits from, an asset or to restrict the access of other entities to those benefits. Therefore, the transfer of legal title of an asset may indicate that the customer has obtained control of the asset. If an entity retains legal title solely as protection against the customer’s failure to pay, those rights of the entity would not preclude the customer from obtaining control of an asset.\\\\n(c)The entity has transferred physical possession of the asset—the customer’s physical possession of an asset may indicate that the customer has the ability to direct the use of, and obtain substantially all of the remaining benefits from, the asset or to restrict the access of other entities to those benefits. However, physical possession may not coincide with control of an asset. For example, in some repurchase agreements and in some consignment arrangements, a customer or consignee may have physical possession of an asset that the entity controls. Conversely, in some bill-and-hold arrangements, the entity may have physical possession of an asset that the customer controls. Paragraphs B64⁠–⁠B76, B77⁠–⁠B78 and B79⁠–⁠B82 provide guidance on accounting for repurchase agreements, consignment arrangements and bill-and-hold arrangements, respectively.\\\\n(d)The customer has the significant risks and rewards of ownership of the asset—the transfer of the significant risks and rewards of ownership of an asset to the customer may indicate that the customer has obtained the ability to direct the use of, and obtain substantially all of the remaining benefits from, the asset. However, when evaluating the risks and rewards of ownership of a promised asset, an entity shall exclude any risks that give rise to a separate performance obligation in addition to the performance obligation to transfer the asset. For example, an entity may have transferred control of an asset to a customer but not yet satisfied an additional performance obligation to provide maintenance services related to the transferred asset.\\\\n(e)The customer has accepted the asset—the customer’s acceptance of an asset may indicate that it has obtained the ability to direct the use of, and obtain substantially all of the remaining benefits from the asset. To evaluate the effect of a contractual customer acceptance clause on when control of an asset is transferred, an entity shall consider the guidance in paragraphs B83⁠–⁠B86.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    }\\n  ]\\n}\"}","expires":1778936405166}

Comment déterminer si le chiffre d’affaires correspondant est comptabilisé à une date donnée ou sur la durée du droit concédé ?:{"id":"exec:uv run python /Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/scripts/run_answer.py","config":{"policy-config":"./effective/policy.default.yaml","llm_provider":"openai-codex","basePath":"/Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/experiments/57_answer-evals_on_variants_for_Q5_Q6_Q9_Q12_Q16/runs/2026-05-02_12-57-25_promptfoo-eval-family-q9"},"env":{}}

cache.value: {"value":"{\"output\":\"{\\n  \\\"assumptions_fr\\\": [\\n    \\\"On suppose que la licence est concédée à un client dans le champ d’IFRS 15.\\\",\\n    \\\"On suppose que la promesse de licence est distincte des autres biens ou services éventuels du contrat ; sinon, le calendrier de comptabilisation s’apprécie pour l’obligation de prestation unique incluant la licence.\\\",\\n    \\\"Les faits fournis ne précisent pas si le client reçoit un droit d’accès ou un droit d’utilisation ; la conclusion dépend donc des caractéristiques contractuelles et des activités attendues de l’entité.\\\"\\n  ],\\n  \\\"recommendation\\\": {\\n    \\\"answer\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n    \\\"justification\\\": \\\"Sous IFRS 15, il faut d’abord déterminer si la licence distincte confère un droit d’accès ou un droit d’utilisation (IFRS 15 B56). Le chiffre d’affaires est comptabilisé sur la durée si les critères de droit d’accès sont remplis (IFRS 15 B58, B60), sinon à une date donnée pour un droit d’utilisation (IFRS 15 B61).\\\"\\n  },\\n  \\\"approaches\\\": [\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_1\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"right_to_access_intellectual_property\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Droit d’accès à la propriété intellectuelle\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, ce traitement s’applique seulement si la licence distincte donne au client un accès à la propriété intellectuelle pendant toute la période de licence, et non un simple droit d’utiliser un actif figé à l’octroi (IFRS 15 B56). Cela suppose que le contrat impose, ou que le client s’attende raisonnablement à, des activités de l’entité qui affectent significativement la propriété intellectuelle, que le client soit exposé aux effets de ces activités et que ces activités ne constituent pas un bien ou service distinct transféré au client (IFRS 15 B58, IFRS 15 25). Si ces critères sont remplis, le produit est reconnu sur la durée car le client reçoit et consomme simultanément le bénéfice de l’accès (IFRS 15 B60, IFRS 15 35(a)).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"La licence est distincte des autres promesses du contrat.\\\",\\n        \\\"Le client s’attend raisonnablement à des activités de l’entité qui affectent significativement la propriété intellectuelle.\\\",\\n        \\\"Ces activités exposent directement le client à leurs effets positifs ou négatifs.\\\",\\n        \\\"Ces activités ne transfèrent pas au fur et à mesure un bien ou un service distinct au client.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Le chiffre d’affaires est comptabilisé sur la durée de la licence, selon une méthode de progression appropriée.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs15\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B56\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If the promise to grant the licence is distinct from the other promised goods or services in the contract and, therefore, the promise to grant the licence is a separate performance obligation, an entity shall determine whether the licence transfers to a customer either at a point in time or over time. In making this determination, an entity shall consider whether the nature of the entity’s promise in granting the licence to a customer is to provide the customer with either: **(a)a right to access the entity’s intellectual property as it exists throughout the licence period;** or (b)a right to use the entity’s intellectual property as it exists at the point in time at which the licence is granted.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs15\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B58\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"The nature of an entity’s promise in granting a licence is a promise to provide a right to access the entity’s intellectual property if **all of the following criteria are met:** **(a)the contract requires, or the customer reasonably expects, that the entity will undertake activities that significantly affect the intellectual property** to which the customer has rights (see paragraphs B59 and B59A); **(b)the rights granted by the licence directly expose the customer to any positive or negative effects of the entity’s activities** identified in paragraph B58(a) ; and **(c)those activities do not result in the transfer of a good or a service to the customer as those activities occur** (see paragraph 25).\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs15\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"25\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Performance obligations do not include activities that an entity must undertake to fulfil a contract unless **those activities transfer a good or service to a customer**. For example, a services provider may need to perform various administrative tasks to set up a contract. The performance of those tasks does not transfer a service to the customer as the tasks are performed. Therefore, those setup activities are not a performance obligation. E2\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs15\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B60\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If the criteria in paragraph B58 are met, an entity shall account for the promise to grant a licence as **a performance obligation satisfied over time because the customer will simultaneously receive and consume the benefit** from the entity’s performance of providing access to its intellectual property as the performance occurs (see paragraph 35(a)). An entity shall apply paragraphs 39⁠–⁠45 to select an appropriate method to measure its progress towards complete satisfaction of that performance obligation to provide access.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs15\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"35\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"An entity transfers control of a good or service over time and, therefore, satisfies a performance obligation and recognises revenue over time, if one of the following criteria is met: E4, E5 **(a)the customer simultaneously receives and consumes the benefits provided by the entity’s performance as the entity performs** (see paragraphs B3⁠–⁠B4); (b)the entity’s performance creates or enhances an asset (for example, work in progress) that the customer controls as the asset is created or enhanced (see paragraph B5); or (c)the entity’s performance does not create an asset with an alternative use to the entity (see paragraph 36) and the entity has an enforceable right to payment for performance completed to date (see paragraph 37).\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_2\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"right_to_use_intellectual_property\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Droit d’utilisation de la propriété intellectuelle\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, ce traitement s’applique si les critères du droit d’accès ne sont pas remplis et que la licence distincte permet au client d’utiliser la propriété intellectuelle telle qu’elle existe au moment de l’octroi (IFRS 15 B56, IFRS 15 B61). IFRS 15 précise alors que l’obligation de prestation est satisfaite à une date donnée, sous réserve que le client puisse effectivement utiliser et tirer avantage de la licence ; le produit ne peut donc pas être reconnu avant le début de la période d’usage ou avant mise à disposition du code ou de l’accès nécessaire (IFRS 15 B61). La date précise se détermine par les indicateurs de transfert du contrôle (IFRS 15 38).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"La licence est distincte des autres promesses du contrat.\\\",\\n        \\\"Les critères du droit d’accès prévus par IFRS 15 B58 ne sont pas remplis.\\\",\\n        \\\"Le client peut effectivement utiliser et tirer avantage de la licence à la date retenue.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Le chiffre d’affaires est comptabilisé à une date donnée, au moment où le contrôle de la licence est transféré et où le client peut l’utiliser.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs15\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B56\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If the promise to grant the licence is distinct from the other promised goods or services in the contract and, therefore, the promise to grant the licence is a separate performance obligation, an entity shall determine whether the licence transfers to a customer either at a point in time or over time. In making this determination, an entity shall consider whether the nature of the entity’s promise in granting the licence to a customer is to provide the customer with either: (a)a right to access the entity’s intellectual property as it exists throughout the licence period; or **(b)a right to use the entity’s intellectual property as it exists at the point in time at which the licence is granted.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs15\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B61\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If the criteria in paragraph B58 are not met, **the nature of an entity’s promise is to provide a right to use the entity’s intellectual property as that intellectual property exists (in terms of form and functionality) at the point in time at which the licence is granted to the customer.** This means that the customer can direct the use of, and obtain substantially all of the remaining benefits from, the licence at the point in time at which the licence transfers. **An entity shall account for the promise to provide a right to use the entity’s intellectual property as a performance obligation satisfied at a point in time.** An entity shall apply paragraph 38 to determine the point in time at which the licence transfers to the customer. **However, revenue cannot be recognised for a licence that provides a right to use the entity’s intellectual property before the beginning of the period during which the customer is able to use and benefit from the licence.** For example, if a software licence period begins before an entity provides (or otherwise makes available) to the customer a code that enables the customer to immediately use the software, the entity would not recognise revenue before that code has been provided (or otherwise made available).\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs15\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"38\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If a performance obligation is not satisfied over time in accordance with paragraphs 35⁠–⁠37, **an entity satisfies the performance obligation at a point in time.** To determine the point in time at which a customer obtains control of a promised asset and the entity satisfies a performance obligation, the entity shall consider the requirements for control in paragraphs 31⁠–⁠34. In addition, an entity shall consider indicators of the transfer of control, which include, but are not limited to, the following: (a)The entity has a present right to payment for the asset—if a customer is presently obliged to pay for an asset, then that may indicate that the customer has obtained the ability to direct the use of, and obtain substantially all of the remaining benefits from, the asset in exchange. (b)The customer has legal title to the asset—legal title may indicate which party to a contract has the ability to direct the use of, and obtain substantially all of the remaining benefits from, an asset or to restrict the access of other entities to those benefits. Therefore, the transfer of legal title of an asset may indicate that the customer has obtained control of the asset. If an entity retains legal title solely as protection against the customer’s failure to pay, those rights of the entity would not preclude the customer from obtaining control of an asset. (c)The entity has transferred physical possession of the asset—the customer’s physical possession of an asset may indicate that the customer has the ability to direct the use of, and obtain substantially all of the remaining benefits from, the asset or to restrict the access of other entities to those benefits. However, physical possession may not coincide with control of an asset. For example, in some repurchase agreements and in some consignment arrangements, a customer or consignee may have physical possession of an asset that the entity controls. Conversely, in some bill-and-hold arrangements, the entity may have physical possession of an asset that the customer controls. Paragraphs B64⁠–⁠B76, B77⁠–⁠B78 and B79⁠–⁠B82 provide guidance on accounting for repurchase agreements, consignment arrangements and bill-and-hold arrangements, respectively. (d)The customer has the significant risks and rewards of ownership of the asset—the transfer of the significant risks and rewards of ownership of an asset to the customer may indicate that the customer has obtained the ability to direct the use of, and obtain substantially all of the remaining benefits from, the asset. However, when evaluating the risks and rewards of ownership of a promised asset, an entity shall exclude any risks that give rise to a separate performance obligation in addition to the performance obligation to transfer the asset. For example, an entity may have transferred control of an asset to a customer but not yet satisfied an additional performance obligation to provide maintenance services related to the transferred asset. (e)The customer has accepted the asset—the customer’s acceptance of an asset may indicate that it has obtained the ability to direct the use of, and obtain substantially all of the remaining benefits from, the asset. To evaluate the effect of a contractual customer acceptance clause on when control of an asset is transferred, an entity shall consider the guidance in paragraphs B83⁠–⁠B86.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    }\\n  ]\\n}\"}","expires":1778936421958}

Comment déterminer si le chiffre d’affaires correspondant est comptabilisé à une date donnée ou sur la durée du droit concédé ?:{"id":"exec:uv run python /Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/scripts/run_answer.py","config":{"policy-config":"./effective/policy.default.yaml","llm_provider":"openai-codex","basePath":"/Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/experiments/57_answer-evals_on_variants_for_Q5_Q6_Q9_Q12_Q16/runs/2026-05-02_12-57-25_promptfoo-eval-family-q9"},"env":{}}

cache.value: {"value":"{\"output\":\"{\\n  \\\"assumptions_fr\\\": [\\n    \\\"On suppose que la licence de propriété intellectuelle constitue une promesse distincte; si elle n’est pas distincte d’autres biens ou services, elle doit être comptabilisée avec l’obligation de prestation combinée selon le modèle général d’IFRS 15 (IFRS 15 B54–B55).\\\",\\n    \\\"Aucun fait supplémentaire n’est fourni sur les activités continues de l’entité, les attentes raisonnables du client, ni sur la date à laquelle la licence devient utilisable par le client.\\\"\\n  ],\\n  \\\"recommendation\\\": {\\n    \\\"answer\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n    \\\"justification\\\": \\\"Le traitement dépend de la nature de la promesse attachée à la licence. Si la licence donne un droit d’accès à la propriété intellectuelle, le chiffre d’affaires est comptabilisé sur la durée; si elle donne un droit d’utilisation, il est comptabilisé à une date donnée (IFRS 15 B56, B60, B61).\\\"\\n  },\\n  \\\"approaches\\\": [\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_1\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"right_to_access_intellectual_property\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Droit d’accès à la propriété intellectuelle\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Ce traitement s’applique dans cette situation seulement si la licence distincte donne au client un accès à la propriété intellectuelle telle qu’elle existe tout au long de la période de licence (IFRS 15 B56). Il faut que le contrat impose, ou que le client s’attende raisonnablement à, des activités de l’entité qui affectent significativement cette propriété intellectuelle, que le client soit exposé directement aux effets de ces activités, et que ces activités ne transfèrent pas un bien ou service distinct (IFRS 15 B58, IFRS 15 25). Dans ce cas, le produit est reconnu sur la durée car le client reçoit et consomme simultanément les bénéfices de l’accès fourni (IFRS 15 B60, IFRS 15 35(a)).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"La licence est distincte des autres biens ou services promis.\\\",\\n        \\\"L’entité mène, ou le client s’attend raisonnablement à ce qu’elle mène, des activités qui affectent significativement la propriété intellectuelle.\\\",\\n        \\\"Ces activités n’aboutissent pas au transfert d’un bien ou service au client au fur et à mesure.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Le chiffre d’affaires est comptabilisé sur la durée du droit concédé, selon une méthode mesurant l’avancement de l’obligation de fournir l’accès.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs15\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B56\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If the promise to grant the licence is distinct from the other promised goods or services in the contract and, therefore, the promise to grant the licence is a separate performance obligation, an entity shall determine whether the licence transfers to a customer either at a point in time or over time. In making this determination, an entity shall consider whether the nature of the entity’s promise in granting the licence to a customer is to provide the customer with either: **(a)a right to access the entity’s intellectual property as it exists throughout the licence period**; or (b)a right to use the entity’s intellectual property as it exists at the point in time at which the licence is granted.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs15\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B58\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"The nature of an entity’s promise in granting a licence is a promise to provide **a right to access the entity’s intellectual property if all of the following criteria are met**: **(a)the contract requires, or the customer reasonably expects, that the entity will undertake activities that significantly affect the intellectual property** to which the customer has rights (see paragraphs B59 and B59A); **(b)the rights granted by the licence directly expose the customer to any positive or negative effects of the entity’s activities** identified in paragraph B58(a) ; and **(c)those activities do not result in the transfer of a good or a service to the customer as those activities occur** (see paragraph 25).\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs15\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"25\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Performance obligations do not include activities that an entity must undertake to fulfil a contract unless **those activities transfer a good or service to a customer**. For example, a services provider may need to perform various administrative tasks to set up a contract. The performance of those tasks does not transfer a service to the customer as the tasks are performed. Therefore, those setup activities are not a performance obligation.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs15\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B60\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If the criteria in paragraph B58 are met, an entity shall account for the promise to grant a licence as **a performance obligation satisfied over time** because the customer will simultaneously receive and consume the benefit from the entity’s performance of providing access to its intellectual property as the performance occurs (see paragraph 35(a)). An entity shall apply paragraphs 39⁠–⁠45 to select an appropriate method to measure its progress towards complete satisfaction of that performance obligation to provide access.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs15\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"35\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"An entity transfers control of a good or service over time and, therefore, satisfies a performance obligation and recognises revenue over time, if one of the following criteria is met: **(a)the customer simultaneously receives and consumes the benefits provided by the entity’s performance as the entity performs** (see paragraphs B3⁠–⁠B4); (b)the entity’s performance creates or enhances an asset (for example, work in progress) that the customer controls as the asset is created or enhanced (see paragraph B5); or (c)the entity’s performance does not create an asset with an alternative use to the entity (see paragraph 36) and the entity has an enforceable right to payment for performance completed to date (see paragraph 37).\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_2\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"right_to_use_intellectual_property\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Droit d’utilisation de la propriété intellectuelle\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Ce traitement s’applique dans cette situation si les critères du droit d’accès ne sont pas remplis et que la licence distincte donne au client un droit d’utiliser la propriété intellectuelle telle qu’elle existe à la date d’octroi (IFRS 15 B56, B61). Dans ce cas, l’obligation est satisfaite à une date donnée, au moment où le contrôle de la licence est transféré au client selon les indicateurs généraux d’IFRS 15 38. Toutefois, aucun produit ne peut être comptabilisé avant le début de la période pendant laquelle le client peut effectivement utiliser et tirer bénéfice de la licence (IFRS 15 B61).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"La licence est distincte des autres biens ou services promis.\\\",\\n        \\\"Les critères du droit d’accès prévus par IFRS 15 B58 ne sont pas remplis.\\\",\\n        \\\"La licence est mise à disposition de sorte que le client puisse immédiatement l’utiliser et en tirer bénéfice.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Le chiffre d’affaires est comptabilisé à une date donnée, au transfert du contrôle de la licence, sans anticipation avant sa mise à disposition effective.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs15\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B56\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If the promise to grant the licence is distinct from the other promised goods or services in the contract and, therefore, the promise to grant the licence is a separate performance obligation, an entity shall determine whether the licence transfers to a customer either at a point in time or over time. In making this determination, an entity shall consider whether the nature of the entity’s promise in granting the licence to a customer is to provide the customer with either: (a)a right to access the entity’s intellectual property as it exists throughout the licence period; or **(b)a right to use the entity’s intellectual property as it exists at the point in time at which the licence is granted**.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs15\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"B61\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If the criteria in paragraph B58 are not met, the nature of an entity’s promise is to provide **a right to use the entity’s intellectual property as that intellectual property exists (in terms of form and functionality) at the point in time at which the licence is granted to the customer**. This means that the customer can direct the use of, and obtain substantially all of the remaining benefits from, the licence at the point in time at which the licence transfers. An entity shall account for the promise to provide a right to use the entity’s intellectual property as **a performance obligation satisfied at a point in time**. An entity shall apply paragraph 38 to determine the point in time at which the licence transfers to the customer. However, **revenue cannot be recognised for a licence that provides a right to use the entity’s intellectual property before the beginning of the period during which the customer is able to use and benefit from the licence**. For example, if a software licence period begins before an entity provides (or otherwise makes available) to the customer a code that enables the customer to immediately use the software, the entity would not recognise revenue before that code has been provided (or otherwise made available).\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ifrs15\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"38\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"If a performance obligation is not satisfied over time in accordance with paragraphs 35⁠–⁠37, **an entity satisfies the performance obligation at a point in time**. To determine the point in time at which a customer obtains control of a promised asset and the entity satisfies a performance obligation, the entity shall consider the requirements for control in paragraphs 31⁠–⁠34. In addition, an entity shall consider indicators of the transfer of control, which include, but are not limited to, the following: **(a)The entity has a present right to payment for the asset**—if a customer is presently obliged to pay for an asset, then that may indicate that the customer has obtained the ability to direct the use of, and obtain substantially all of the remaining benefits from, the asset in exchange. **(b)The customer has legal title to the asset**—legal title may indicate which party to a contract has the ability to direct the use of, and obtain substantially all of the remaining benefits from, an asset or to restrict the access of other entities to those benefits. Therefore, the transfer of legal title of an asset may indicate that the customer has obtained control of the asset. If an entity retains legal title solely as protection against the customer’s failure to pay, those rights of the entity would not preclude the customer from obtaining control of an asset. **(c)The entity has transferred physical possession of the asset**—the customer’s physical possession of an asset may indicate that the customer has the ability to direct the use of, and obtain substantially all of the remaining benefits from, the asset or to restrict the access of other entities to those benefits. However, physical possession may not coincide with control of an asset. For example, in some repurchase agreements and in some consignment arrangements, a customer or consignee may have physical possession of an asset that the entity controls. Conversely, in some bill-and-hold arrangements, the entity may have physical possession of an asset that the customer controls. Paragraphs B64⁠–⁠B76, B77⁠–⁠B78 and B79⁠–⁠B82 provide guidance on accounting for repurchase agreements, consignment arrangements and bill-and-hold arrangements, respectively. **(d)The customer has the significant risks and rewards of ownership of the asset**—the transfer of the significant risks and rewards of ownership of an asset to the customer may indicate that the customer has obtained the ability to direct the use of, and obtain substantially all of the remaining benefits from, the asset. However, when evaluating the risks and rewards of ownership of a promised asset, an entity shall exclude any risks that give rise to a separate performance obligation in addition to the performance obligation to transfer the asset. For example, an entity may have transferred control of an asset to a customer but not yet satisfied an additional performance obligation to provide maintenance services related to the transferred asset. **(e)The customer has accepted the asset**—the customer’s acceptance of an asset may indicate that it has obtained the ability to direct the use of, and obtain substantially all of the remaining benefits from, the asset. To evaluate the effect of a contractual customer acceptance clause on when control of an asset is transferred, an entity shall consider the guidance in paragraphs B83⁠–⁠B86.\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    }\\n  ]\\n}\"}","expires":1778936431690}
