Faut-il reconnaître une provision pour cet engagement ou pour l’achat futur de crédits carbone destinés à compenser les dépassements d’émissions ?:{"id":"exec:uv run python /Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/scripts/run_answer.py","config":{"policy-config":"./effective/policy.default.yaml","llm_provider":"openai-codex","basePath":"/Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/experiments/57_answer-evals_on_variants_for_Q5_Q6_Q9_Q12_Q16/runs/2026-05-02_13-12-42_promptfoo-eval-family-q16"},"env":{}}

cache.value: {"value":"{\"output\":\"{\\n  \\\"assumptions_fr\\\": [\\n    \\\"L’engagement est public, volontaire et suffisamment précis quant à l’objectif de neutralité carbone et à l’horizon visé.\\\",\\n    \\\"La question porte sur la comptabilisation au titre d’IAS 37 à une date de clôture antérieure ou concomitante aux émissions futures à compenser.\\\",\\n    \\\"Aucune loi spécifique distincte de l’engagement volontaire n’impose, dans les faits décrits, l’achat de crédits carbone avant que les émissions concernées n’aient eu lieu.\\\"\\n  ],\\n  \\\"recommendation\\\": {\\n    \\\"answer\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n    \\\"justification\\\": \\\"Aucune provision n’est comptabilisée pour le seul engagement public ni pour des achats futurs de crédits carbone destinés à compenser des émissions futures. Une provision n’est reconnue qu’à mesure que les émissions à compenser ont effectivement été générées, et seulement si l’engagement a créé une obligation constructive.\\\"\\n  },\\n  \\\"approaches\\\": [\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_1\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"provision\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Provision\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, IAS 37 n’autorise pas une provision au seul moment de l’annonce de neutralité carbone, car il n’existe pas encore d’obligation actuelle résultant d’un événement passé pour les coûts de réduction ou pour l’achat futur de crédits visant des émissions futures (ias37 14, 18, 19, E6). En revanche, si l’engagement public crée une obligation constructive, une provision devient possible uniquement lorsque les émissions que l’entité s’est engagée à compenser ont effectivement été émises; à ce moment, l’achat et la mise au rebut des crédits entraînent une sortie probable de ressources (ias37 10, 14, E6).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"L’engagement public a créé une obligation constructive au sens d’IAS 37.\\\",\\n        \\\"Les émissions que l’entité s’est engagée à compenser ont déjà été effectivement émises à la date de clôture.\\\",\\n        \\\"L’obligation n’a pas encore été réglée et son montant peut être estimé de manière fiable.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Pas de provision initiale pour la promesse de neutralité carbone ni pour des achats futurs de crédits; provision seulement au titre des émissions passées à compenser.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ias37\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"14\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"A provision shall be recognised when: **(a)an entity has a present obligation (legal or constructive) as a result of a past event; (b)it is probable that an outflow of resources embodying economic benefits will be required to settle the obligation; and (c)a reliable estimate can be made of the amount of the obligation. If these conditions are not met, no provision shall be recognised.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ias37\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"18\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Financial statements deal with the financial position of an entity at the end of its reporting period and not its possible position in the future. Therefore, **no provision is recognised for costs that need to be incurred to operate in the future.** The only liabilities recognised in an entity’s statement of financial position are those that exist at the end of the reporting period.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ias37\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"19\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"It is only those obligations arising from past events existing independently of an entity’s future actions (ie the future conduct of its business) that are recognised as provisions. ... **Because the entity can avoid the future expenditure by its future actions... it has no present obligation for that future expenditure and no provision is recognised.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ias37\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"10\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"The following terms are used in this Standard with the meanings specified: ... **A constructive obligation is an obligation that derives from an entity’s actions where: (a)by an established pattern of past practice, published policies or a sufficiently specific current statement, the entity has indicated to other parties that it will accept certain responsibilities; and (b)as a result, the entity has created a valid expectation on the part of those other parties that it will discharge those responsibilities.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ias37\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"E6\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"[IFRIC® Update, March 2024, Agenda Decision, ‘ IAS 37 Provisions, Contingent Liabilities and Contingent Assets —Climate-related Commitments’ + ... **the entity does not recognise a provision when it makes the statement in 20X0. At that time, the constructive obligation is not a present obligation as a result of a past event.** ... **the entity does not recognise a provision between 20X0 and 20X9 because it does not have a present obligation as a result of a past event until it has emitted the greenhouse gases it has committed to offset.** ... **as the entity emits greenhouse gases in 20X9 and in subsequent years, it will incur a present obligation to offset these past emissions. If the entity has not yet settled that obligation and a reliable estimate can be made of the amount of the obligation, the entity recognises a provision.** ... **settling the constructive obligation to offset the entity’s remaining annual greenhouse gas emissions will require an outflow of resources embodying economic benefits. The entity will be required to buy and retire carbon credits without receiving any resources embodying economic benefits in exchange.**]\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_2\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"contingent_liability\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Passif éventuel\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, si l’engagement public n’a pas encore créé d’obligation actuelle à la date de clôture, ou si l’existence même d’une obligation constructive reste seulement possible, le traitement relève d’un passif éventuel et non d’une provision (ias37 10, 13, 16, 23, E6). Cela vise en pratique la période antérieure aux émissions à compenser, sauf si la possibilité de sortie est éloignée.\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"À la date de clôture, l’existence d’une obligation actuelle demeure seulement possible ou plus improbable que probable.\\\",\\n        \\\"Ou bien une obligation existe mais ne remplit pas les critères de comptabilisation d’une provision.\\\",\\n        \\\"La possibilité de sortie de ressources n’est pas éloignée.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Absence de provision; information éventuelle en annexe comme passif éventuel selon l’appréciation des faits à la date de clôture.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ias37\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"10\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"The following terms are used in this Standard with the meanings specified: ... **A contingent liability is: (a)a possible obligation that arises from past events and whose existence will be confirmed only by the occurrence or non‑occurrence of one or more uncertain future events not wholly within the control of the entity; or (b)a present obligation that arises from past events but is not recognised because: (i)it is not probable that an outflow of resources embodying economic benefits will be required to settle the obligation; or (ii)the amount of the obligation cannot be measured with sufficient reliability.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ias37\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"13\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"This Standard distinguishes between: **(a)provisions – which are recognised as liabilities** ... and **(b)contingent liabilities – which are not recognised as liabilities because they are either: (i)possible obligations...; or (ii)present obligations that do not meet the recognition criteria in this Standard**...\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ias37\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"16\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"On the basis of such evidence: **(a)where it is more likely than not that a present obligation exists at the end of the reporting period, the entity recognises a provision (if the recognition criteria are met); and (b)where it is more likely that no present obligation exists at the end of the reporting period, the entity discloses a contingent liability, unless the possibility of an outflow of resources embodying economic benefits is remote**...\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ias37\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"23\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"For the purpose of this Standard, **an outflow of resources or other event is regarded as probable if the event is more likely than not to occur**... **Where it is not probable that a present obligation exists, an entity discloses a contingent liability, unless the possibility of an outflow of resources embodying economic benefits is remote**...\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ias37\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"E6\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"[IFRIC® Update, March 2024, Agenda Decision, ‘ IAS 37 Provisions, Contingent Liabilities and Contingent Assets —Climate-related Commitments’ + ... **whether the entity’s statement of its commitments to reduce and offset its greenhouse gas emissions creates a constructive obligation to fulfil those commitments will depend on the facts of the statement and the circumstances surrounding it.** ... **if the statement creates a constructive obligation... the entity does not recognise a provision when it makes the statement in 20X0.**]\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    }\\n  ]\\n}\"}","expires":1778937410551}

cache: keyv:repeat:0:exec:uv run python /Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/scripts/run_answer.py:4606dc2dc0f5c27588a548eb91f4dfc4f2d83a6b2066151c169a0cb3228e57d0:Dans quelle mesure un engagement volontaire de contribution à la neutralité carbone crée-t-il une obligation actuelle imposant la comptabilisation d’une provision pour les crédits carbone à utiliser ultérieurement ?:{"id":"exec:uv run python /Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/scripts/run_answer.py","config":{"policy-config":"./effective/policy.default.yaml","llm_provider":"openai-codex","basePath":"/Users/philippe/Documents/workspace/ifrs-expert/experiments/57_answer-evals_on_variants_for_Q5_Q6_Q9_Q12_Q16/runs/2026-05-02_13-12-42_promptfoo-eval-family-q16"},"env":{}}

cache.value: {"value":"{\"output\":\"{\\n  \\\"assumptions_fr\\\": [\\n    \\\"L’engagement de contribution à la neutralité carbone est volontaire et public, et vise l’achat futur de crédits carbone pour compenser des émissions futures.\\\",\\n    \\\"La question porte sur la date de l’engagement et sur les périodes antérieures aux émissions à compenser, puis sur la période où les émissions sont effectivement produites.\\\",\\n    \\\"Aucune obligation légale distincte n’est supposée ; l’analyse porte donc principalement sur une éventuelle obligation implicite au sens d’IAS 37.\\\"\\n  ],\\n  \\\"recommendation\\\": {\\n    \\\"answer\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n    \\\"justification\\\": \\\"En IAS 37, l’engagement volontaire ne conduit pas, à lui seul, à une provision pour des crédits carbone futurs. Une provision n’est envisageable que si l’engagement crée une obligation implicite et seulement au fur et à mesure que les émissions que l’entité s’est engagée à compenser ont déjà été émises.\\\"\\n  },\\n  \\\"approaches\\\": [\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_1\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"provision\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Provision\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, IAS 37 exclut une provision au seul moment de l’engagement, même public, car il ne crée pas encore une obligation actuelle résultant d’un événement passé ; les coûts de réduction et de compensation future restent des coûts d’exploitation futurs (IAS 37 18, 19, E6). Une provision pour crédits carbone n’est possible que si l’engagement a créé une obligation implicite et seulement lorsque les émissions à compenser ont effectivement été générées ; à ce moment, l’achat et le retrait de crédits entraînent une sortie de ressources probable et estimable (IAS 37 10, 23, 25, E6).\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"L’engagement public doit avoir créé une obligation implicite au sens d’IAS 37.\\\",\\n        \\\"Les émissions que l’entité s’est engagée à compenser doivent déjà avoir été émises à la date de clôture.\\\",\\n        \\\"L’obligation de compensation ne doit pas encore être réglée à la date de clôture.\\\",\\n        \\\"Le montant de l’obligation doit pouvoir être estimé de façon fiable.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Pas de provision à la date de l’annonce ; provision seulement au fur et à mesure des émissions effectivement soumises à compensation future.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ias37\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"10\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"The following terms are used in this Standard with the meanings specified:\\\\nA provision is a liability of uncertain timing or amount.\\\\nA liability is a present obligation of the entity arising from past events, the settlement of which is expected to result in an outflow from the entity of resources embodying economic benefits. 1\\\\nAn obligating event is an event that creates a legal or constructive obligation that results in an entity having **no realistic alternative to settling that obligation**.\\\\nA legal obligation is an obligation that derives from:\\\\n(a)a contract (through its explicit or implicit terms);\\\\n(b)legislation; or\\\\n(c)other operation of law.\\\\nA constructive obligation is an obligation that derives from an entity’s actions where:\\\\n(a)by an established pattern of past practice, published policies or a sufficiently specific current statement, the entity has indicated to other parties that it will accept certain responsibilities; and\\\\n(b)as a result, the entity has created a valid expectation on the part of those other parties that it will discharge those responsibilities.\\\\nA contingent liability is:\\\\n(a)a possible obligation that arises from past events and whose existence will be confirmed only by the occurrence or non‑occurrence of one or more uncertain future events not wholly within the control of the entity; or\\\\n(b)a present obligation that arises from past events but is not recognised because:\\\\n(i)it is not probable that an outflow of resources embodying economic benefits will be required to settle the obligation; or\\\\n(ii)the amount of the obligation cannot be measured with sufficient reliability.\\\\nA contingent asset is a possible asset that arises from past events and whose existence will be confirmed only by the occurrence or non‑occurrence of one or more uncertain future events not wholly within the control of the entity. E4\\\\nAn onerous contract is a contract in which the unavoidable costs of meeting the obligations under the contract exceed the economic benefits expected to be received under it.\\\\nA restructuring is a programme that is planned and controlled by management, and materially changes either:\\\\n(a)the scope of a business undertaken by an entity; or\\\\n(b)the manner in which that business is conducted.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ias37\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"18\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"Financial statements deal with the financial position of an entity at the end of its reporting period and not its possible position in the future. Therefore, **no provision is recognised for costs that need to be incurred to operate in the future**. The only liabilities recognised in an entity’s statement of financial position are those that exist at the end of the reporting period.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ias37\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"19\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"It is only those obligations arising from past events existing independently of an entity’s future actions (ie the future conduct of its business) that are recognised as provisions. Examples of such obligations are penalties or clean‑up costs for unlawful environmental damage, both of which would lead to an outflow of resources embodying economic benefits in settlement regardless of the future actions of the entity. Similarly, an entity recognises a provision for the decommissioning costs of an oil installation or a nuclear power station to the extent that the entity is obliged to rectify damage already caused. In contrast, because of commercial pressures or legal requirements, an entity may intend or need to carry out expenditure to operate in a particular way in the future (for example, by fitting smoke filters in a certain type of factory). **Because the entity can avoid the future expenditure by its future actions**, for example by changing its method of operation, **it has no present obligation for that future expenditure and no provision is recognised**. E5, E6\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ias37\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"23\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"For a liability to qualify for recognition there must be not only a present obligation but also the probability of an outflow of resources embodying economic benefits to settle that obligation. For the purpose of this Standard, 2 **an outflow of resources or other event is regarded as probable if the event is more likely than not to occur**, ie the probability that the event will occur is greater than the probability that it will not. Where it is not probable that a present obligation exists, an entity discloses a contingent liability, unless the possibility of an outflow of resources embodying economic benefits is remote (see paragraph 86).\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ias37\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"25\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"The use of estimates is an essential part of the preparation of financial statements and does not undermine their reliability. This is especially true in the case of provisions, which by their nature are more uncertain than most other items in the statement of financial position. **Except in extremely rare cases, an entity will be able to determine a range of possible outcomes and can therefore make an estimate of the obligation that is sufficiently reliable to use in recognising a provision.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ias37\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"E6\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"[IFRIC® Update, March 2024, Agenda Decision, ‘ IAS 37 Provisions, Contingent Liabilities and Contingent Assets —Climate-related Commitments’ +\\\\nThe Committee received a request asking it to clarify:\\\\na.whether an entity’s commitment to reduce or offset its greenhouse gas emissions creates a constructive obligation for the entity;\\\\nb.whether a constructive obligation created by such a commitment meets the criteria in IAS 37 for recognising a provision; and\\\\nc.if a provision is recognised, whether the corresponding amount is recognised as an expense or as an asset when the provision is recognised.\\\\nThe Committee considered this request by reference to the following fact pattern.\\\\nFact pattern\\\\nIn 20X0 an entity, a manufacturer of household products, publicly states its commitment:\\\\na.to gradually reduce its annual greenhouse gas emissions, reducing them by at least 60% of their current level by 20X9; and\\\\nb.to offset its remaining annual emissions in 20X9 and in subsequent years by buying carbon credits and retiring them from the carbon market.\\\\nTo support its statement, the entity publishes a transition plan setting out how it will gradually modify its manufacturing methods between 20X1 and 20X9 to achieve the 60% reduction in its annual emissions by 20X9. The modifications will involve investing in more energy-efficient processes, buying energy from renewable sources and replacing existing petroleum-based product ingredients and packaging materials with lower-carbon alternatives. Management is confident that the entity can make all these modifications and continue to sell its products at a profit.\\\\nIn addition to publishing the transition plan, the entity takes several other actions that publicly affirm its intention to fulfil its commitments.\\\\nDoes the entity have a constructive obligation?\\\\nParagraph 10 of IAS 37 defines a constructive obligation as an obligation that derives from an entity’s actions where:\\\\na.by an established pattern of past practice, published policies or a sufficiently specific current statement, the entity has indicated to other parties that it will accept certain responsibilities; and\\\\nb.as a result, the entity has created a valid expectation on the part of those other parties that it will discharge those responsibilities.\\\\nParagraph 20 of IAS 37 states that an obligation always involves another party to whom the obligation is owed, but that it is not necessary to know the identity of that party—the obligation may be to the public at large. The Committee observed that a constructive obligation to reduce or offset greenhouse gas emissions, if one exists, would be owed to all people adversely affected by the emissions so would extend to the public at large. The Committee further observed that whether an entity’s statement of its commitment to reduce or offset its emissions creates a valid expectation that it will fulfil the commitment—and hence creates a constructive obligation to do so—depends on the facts of the commitment and the circumstances surrounding it, including any actions the entity has taken that publicly affirm its intention to fulfil the commitment. Management would apply judgement to reach a conclusion at each reporting date considering all relevant facts and circumstances existing at that date. If the facts or circumstances change from one reporting date to the next, so too could the conclusion.\\\\nIf the entity’s statement has not created a constructive obligation, the entity does not recognise a provision. If the entity’s statement has created a constructive obligation, the next question to consider is whether that obligation satisfies the criteria for recognising a provision.\\\\nDoes the constructive obligation satisfy the criteria for recognising a provision?\\\\nParagraph 14 of IAS 37 requires an entity to recognise a provision when:\\\\na.the entity has a present obligation (legal or constructive) as a result of a past event;\\\\nb.it is probable that an outflow of resources embodying economic benefits will be required to settle the obligation; and\\\\nc.a reliable estimate can be made of the amount of the obligation.\\\\nAn entity recognises a provision only if all three of these recognition criteria are met.\\\\nPresent obligation as a result of a past event\\\\nThe first criterion for recognising a provision is that the entity has a present obligation as a result of a past event.\\\\nThe Committee observed that, just as the enactment of a law is not sufficient to give an entity a present legal obligation, **the publication of a policy or statement is not sufficient to give an entity a present constructive obligation—an entity has a present legal or constructive obligation only when the event to which the law, policy or statement applies has occurred**. For example, as illustrated in Illustrative Example 2B accompanying IAS 37, an entity with a widely published policy of cleaning up land it contaminates incurs a present obligation only when it contaminates land—publishing the policy is necessary but not sufficient to give the entity a present obligation.\\\\nIn explaining the requirement for a present obligation, paragraph 18 of IAS 37 states that ‘no provision is recognised for costs that need to be incurred to operate in the future’ and paragraph 19 of IAS 37 states that ‘it is only those obligations arising from past events existing independently of an entity’s future actions (ie the future conduct of its business) that are recognised as provisions’.\\\\nApplying those paragraphs, the Committee concluded that if the commitments described in the fact pattern creates a constructive obligation for the entity:\\\\na.that obligation is **not a present obligation as a result of a past event when the entity publicly states the commitments in 20X0**. Neither stating a commitment nor taking actions that affirm the entity’s intention to fulfil that commitment are events that create a present obligation. The events that create a present obligation are the events to which the statement applies and those events have not occurred at the time the entity states its commitment. The costs that the entity will incur to reduce its annual greenhouse gas emissions and to offset the greenhouse gases it emits in 20X9 and in subsequent years are **costs that it will need to incur to operate in the future**—the obligations for those costs do not exist independently of the entity’s future actions.\\\\nb.at any given date, the entity does not have a present obligation to reduce its emissions after that date, because the costs of operating with lower emissions in the future will remain costs that need to be incurred to operate in the future. The entity will at some future date have a liability to pay for resources it buys to carry out those future operations—for example, to pay for new plant or equipment—but only when it receives those resources in an exchange transaction.\\\\nc.**only when the entity has emitted the greenhouse gases that it has committed to offset will it have a present obligation to offset those greenhouse gases**. The entity will have that present obligation only if and when it emits greenhouse gases in 20X9 and in subsequent years.\\\\nProbable outflow of resources\\\\nThe second criterion for recognising a provision is that it is probable that an outflow of resources embodying economic benefits will be required to settle the obligation.\\\\nThe Committee concluded that if the commitments described in the fact pattern create a constructive obligation for the entity:\\\\na.settling the constructive obligation to reduce the entity’s annual greenhouse gas emissions will not require an outflow of resources embodying economic benefits. Although the entity will incur expenditure to modify its manufacturing methods, it will receive other resources—for example, property, plant, equipment, energy, product ingredients or packaging materials—in exchange, and will be able to use these resources to manufacture products it can sell at a profit.\\\\nb.**settling the constructive obligation to offset the entity’s remaining annual greenhouse gas emissions will require an outflow of resources embodying economic benefits. The entity will be required to buy and retire carbon credits without receiving any resources embodying economic benefits in exchange.**\\\\nReliable estimate\\\\nThe final criterion for recognising a provision is that a reliable estimate can be made of the amount of the obligation.\\\\nParagraph 25 of IAS 37 states that 'except in extremely rare cases, an entity will be able to determine a range of possible outcomes and can therefore make an estimate of the obligation that is sufficiently reliable to use in recognising a provision’.\\\\nThe Committee concluded that in the fact pattern described, it is likely that the entity would be able to make a reliable estimate of the amount of a constructive obligation that satisfies the other recognition criteria.\\\\nConclusion on whether a provision is recognised\\\\nThe Committee concluded that in the fact pattern described:\\\\na.whether the entity’s statement of its commitments to reduce and offset its greenhouse gas emissions creates a constructive obligation to fulfil those commitments will depend on the facts of the statement and the circumstances surrounding it.\\\\nb.if the statement creates a constructive obligation:\\\\ni.**the entity does not recognise a provision when it makes the statement in 20X0. At that time, the constructive obligation is not a present obligation as a result of a past event.**\\\\nii.**the entity does not recognise a provision between 20X0 and 20X9 because it does not have a present obligation as a result of a past event until it has emitted the greenhouse gases it has committed to offset.**\\\\niii.**as the entity emits greenhouse gases in 20X9 and in subsequent years, it will incur a present obligation to offset these past emissions. If the entity has not yet settled that obligation and a reliable estimate can be made of the amount of the obligation, the entity recognises a provision.**\\\\nIf a provision is recognised, is the corresponding amount recognised as an expense or as an asset when the provision is recognised?\\\\nThe Committee observed that if a provision is recognised, the corresponding amount is recognised as an expense, rather than as an asset, unless it gives rise to—or forms part of the cost of—an item that qualifies for recognition as an asset in accordance with an IFRS Accounting Standard.\\\\nOther accounting and disclosure implications\\\\nThe Committee observed that, irrespective of whether an entity’s commitment to reduce or offset its greenhouse gas emissions results in the recognition of a provision, the actions the entity plans to take to fulfil that commitment could affect the amounts at which it measures its other assets and liabilities and the information it discloses about them, as required by various IFRS Accounting Standards.\\\\nConclusion\\\\nThe Committee concluded that the principles and requirements in IFRS Accounting Standards provide an adequate basis for an entity to determine:\\\\na.whether an entity’s commitment to reduce or offset its greenhouse gas emissions creates a constructive obligation for the entity;\\\\nb.the circumstances in which the entity recognises a provision for the costs of fulfilling a constructive obligation to reduce or offset its greenhouse gas emissions; and\\\\nc.if a provision is recognised, whether the corresponding amount is recognised as an expense or as an asset when the provision is recognised.\\\\nConsequently, the Committee decided not to add a standard-setting project to the work plan.]\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    },\\n    {\\n      \\\"id\\\": \\\"approach_2\\\",\\n      \\\"normalized_label\\\": \\\"contingent_liability\\\",\\n      \\\"label_fr\\\": \\\"Passif éventuel\\\",\\n      \\\"applicability\\\": \\\"oui_sous_conditions\\\",\\n      \\\"reasoning_fr\\\": \\\"Dans cette situation, si l’engagement volontaire n’a pas créé d’obligation implicite valide envers des tiers, il n’y a pas d’obligation actuelle et donc pas de provision ; l’analyse peut alors relever d’un passif éventuel seulement s’il existe une obligation possible ou si l’existence d’une obligation actuelle reste incertaine (IAS 37 10, 15, 16, 23). En revanche, si la possibilité de sortie de ressources est seulement lointaine, même le passif éventuel n’est pas à retenir selon IAS 37 16 et 23.\\\",\\n      \\\"conditions_fr\\\": [\\n        \\\"L’engagement public ne crée pas d’obligation implicite certaine à la date de clôture, ou l’existence d’une obligation actuelle demeure plus probable qu’improbable.\\\",\\n        \\\"La possibilité de sortie de ressources n’est pas lointaine.\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"practical_implication_fr\\\": \\\"Avant les émissions ou en cas d’obligation insuffisamment établie, le traitement approprié peut être l’absence de provision, avec passif éventuel seulement si IAS 37 le justifie.\\\",\\n      \\\"references\\\": [\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ias37\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"10\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"The following terms are used in this Standard with the meanings specified:\\\\nA provision is a liability of uncertain timing or amount.\\\\nA liability is a present obligation of the entity arising from past events, the settlement of which is expected to result in an outflow from the entity of resources embodying economic benefits. 1\\\\nAn obligating event is an event that creates a legal or constructive obligation that results in an entity having no realistic alternative to settling that obligation.\\\\nA legal obligation is an obligation that derives from:\\\\n(a)a contract (through its explicit or implicit terms);\\\\n(b)legislation; or\\\\n(c)other operation of law.\\\\nA constructive obligation is an obligation that derives from an entity’s actions where:\\\\n(a)by an established pattern of past practice, published policies or a sufficiently specific current statement, the entity has indicated to other parties that it will accept certain responsibilities; and\\\\n(b)as a result, the entity has created a valid expectation on the part of those other parties that it will discharge those responsibilities.\\\\nA contingent liability is:\\\\n(a)**a possible obligation** that arises from past events and whose existence will be confirmed only by the occurrence or non‑occurrence of one or more uncertain future events not wholly within the control of the entity; or\\\\n(b)**a present obligation** that arises from past events but is not recognised because:\\\\n(i)it is not probable that an outflow of resources embodying economic benefits will be required to settle the obligation; or\\\\n(ii)the amount of the obligation cannot be measured with sufficient reliability.\\\\nA contingent asset is a possible asset that arises from past events and whose existence will be confirmed only by the occurrence or non‑occurrence of one or more uncertain future events not wholly within the control of the entity. E4\\\\nAn onerous contract is a contract in which the unavoidable costs of meeting the obligations under the contract exceed the economic benefits expected to be received under it.\\\\nA restructuring is a programme that is planned and controlled by management, and materially changes either:\\\\n(a)the scope of a business undertaken by an entity; or\\\\n(b)the manner in which that business is conducted.\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ias37\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"15\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In rare cases it is not clear whether there is a present obligation. In these cases, **a past event is deemed to give rise to a present obligation if, taking account of all available evidence, it is more likely than not that a present obligation exists at the end of the reporting period.**\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ias37\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"16\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"In almost all cases it will be clear whether a past event has given rise to a present obligation. In rare cases, for example in a lawsuit, it may be disputed either whether certain events have occurred or whether those events result in a present obligation. In such a case, an entity determines whether a present obligation exists at the end of the reporting period by taking account of all available evidence, including, for example, the opinion of experts. The evidence considered includes any additional evidence provided by events after the reporting period. On the basis of such evidence:\\\\n(a)where it is more likely than not that a present obligation exists at the end of the reporting period, the entity recognises a provision (if the recognition criteria are met); and\\\\n(b)where it is more likely that **no present obligation exists at the end of the reporting period, the entity discloses a contingent liability, unless the possibility of an outflow of resources embodying economic benefits is remote** (see paragraph 86).\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ias37\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"23\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"For a liability to qualify for recognition there must be not only a present obligation but also the probability of an outflow of resources embodying economic benefits to settle that obligation. For the purpose of this Standard, 2 an outflow of resources or other event is regarded as probable if the event is more likely than not to occur, ie the probability that the event will occur is greater than the probability that it will not. **Where it is not probable that a present obligation exists, an entity discloses a contingent liability, unless the possibility of an outflow of resources embodying economic benefits is remote** (see paragraph 86).\\\"\\n        },\\n        {\\n          \\\"document\\\": \\\"ias37\\\",\\n          \\\"section\\\": \\\"E6\\\",\\n          \\\"excerpt\\\": \\\"[IFRIC® Update, March 2024, Agenda Decision, ‘ IAS 37 Provisions, Contingent Liabilities and Contingent Assets —Climate-related Commitments’ +\\\\nThe Committee received a request asking it to clarify:\\\\na.whether an entity’s commitment to reduce or offset its greenhouse gas emissions creates a constructive obligation for the entity;\\\\nb.whether a constructive obligation created by such a commitment meets the criteria in IAS 37 for recognising a provision; and\\\\nc.if a provision is recognised, whether the corresponding amount is recognised as an expense or as an asset when the provision is recognised.\\\\nThe Committee considered this request by reference to the following fact pattern.\\\\nFact pattern\\\\nIn 20X0 an entity, a manufacturer of household products, publicly states its commitment:\\\\na.to gradually reduce its annual greenhouse gas emissions, reducing them by at least 60% of their current level by 20X9; and\\\\nb.to offset its remaining annual emissions in 20X9 and in subsequent years by buying carbon credits and retiring them from the carbon market.\\\\nTo support its statement, the entity publishes a transition plan setting out how it will gradually modify its manufacturing methods between 20X1 and 20X9 to achieve the 60% reduction in its annual emissions by 20X9. The modifications will involve investing in more energy-efficient processes, buying energy from renewable sources and replacing existing petroleum-based product ingredients and packaging materials with lower-carbon alternatives. Management is confident that the entity can make all these modifications and continue to sell its products at a profit.\\\\nIn addition to publishing the transition plan, the entity takes several other actions that publicly affirm its intention to fulfil its commitments.\\\\nDoes the entity have a constructive obligation?\\\\nParagraph 10 of IAS 37 defines a constructive obligation as an obligation that derives from an entity’s actions where:\\\\na.by an established pattern of past practice, published policies or a sufficiently specific current statement, the entity has indicated to other parties that it will accept certain responsibilities; and\\\\nb.as a result, the entity has created a valid expectation on the part of those other parties that it will discharge those responsibilities.\\\\nParagraph 20 of IAS 37 states that an obligation always involves another party to whom the obligation is owed, but that it is not necessary to know the identity of that party—the obligation may be to the public at large. The Committee observed that a constructive obligation to reduce or offset greenhouse gas emissions, if one exists, would be owed to all people adversely affected by the emissions so would extend to the public at large. The Committee further observed that **whether an entity’s statement of its commitment to reduce or offset its emissions creates a valid expectation that it will fulfil the commitment—and hence creates a constructive obligation to do so—depends on the facts of the commitment and the circumstances surrounding it**, including any actions the entity has taken that publicly affirm its intention to fulfil the commitment. Management would apply judgement to reach a conclusion at each reporting date considering all relevant facts and circumstances existing at that date. If the facts or circumstances change from one reporting date to the next, so too could the conclusion.\\\\nIf the entity’s statement has not created a constructive obligation, **the entity does not recognise a provision**. If the entity’s statement has created a constructive obligation, the next question to consider is whether that obligation satisfies the criteria for recognising a provision.\\\\nDoes the constructive obligation satisfy the criteria for recognising a provision?\\\\nParagraph 14 of IAS 37 requires an entity to recognise a provision when:\\\\na.the entity has a present obligation (legal or constructive) as a result of a past event;\\\\nb.it is probable that an outflow of resources embodying economic benefits will be required to settle the obligation; and\\\\nc.a reliable estimate can be made of the amount of the obligation.\\\\nAn entity recognises a provision only if all three of these recognition criteria are met.\\\\nPresent obligation as a result of a past event\\\\nThe first criterion for recognising a provision is that the entity has a present obligation as a result of a past event.\\\\nThe Committee observed that, just as the enactment of a law is not sufficient to give an entity a present legal obligation, the publication of a policy or statement is not sufficient to give an entity a present constructive obligation—an entity has a present legal or constructive obligation only when the event to which the law, policy or statement applies has occurred. For example, as illustrated in Illustrative Example 2B accompanying IAS 37, an entity with a widely published policy of cleaning up land it contaminates incurs a present obligation only when it contaminates land—publishing the policy is necessary but not sufficient to give the entity a present obligation.\\\\nIn explaining the requirement for a present obligation, paragraph 18 of IAS 37 states that ‘no provision is recognised for costs that need to be incurred to operate in the future’ and paragraph 19 of IAS 37 states that ‘it is only those obligations arising from past events existing independently of an entity’s future actions (ie the future conduct of its business) that are recognised as provisions’.\\\\nApplying those paragraphs, the Committee concluded that if the commitments described in the fact pattern creates a constructive obligation for the entity:\\\\na.that obligation is not a present obligation as a result of a past event when the entity publicly states the commitments in 20X0. Neither stating a commitment nor taking actions that affirm the entity’s intention to fulfil that commitment are events that create a present obligation. The events that create a present obligation are the events to which the statement applies and those events have not occurred at the time the entity states its commitment. The costs that the entity will incur to reduce its annual greenhouse gas emissions and to offset the greenhouse gases it emits in 20X9 and in subsequent years are costs that it will need to incur to operate in the future—the obligations for those costs do not exist independently of the entity’s future actions.\\\\nb.at any given date, the entity does not have a present obligation to reduce its emissions after that date, because the costs of operating with lower emissions in the future will remain costs that need to be incurred to operate in the future. The entity will at some future date have a liability to pay for resources it buys to carry out those future operations—for example, to pay for new plant or equipment—but only when it receives those resources in an exchange transaction.\\\\nc.only when the entity has emitted the greenhouse gases that it has committed to offset will it have a present obligation to offset those greenhouse gases. The entity will have that present obligation only if and when it emits greenhouse gases in 20X9 and in subsequent years.\\\\nProbable outflow of resources\\\\nThe second criterion for recognising a provision is that it is probable that an outflow of resources embodying economic benefits will be required to settle the obligation.\\\\nThe Committee concluded that if the commitments described in the fact pattern create a constructive obligation for the entity:\\\\na.settling the constructive obligation to reduce the entity’s annual greenhouse gas emissions will not require an outflow of resources embodying economic benefits. Although the entity will incur expenditure to modify its manufacturing methods, it will receive other resources—for example, property, plant, equipment, energy, product ingredients or packaging materials—in exchange, and will be able to use these resources to manufacture products it can sell at a profit.\\\\nb.settling the constructive obligation to offset the entity’s remaining annual greenhouse gas emissions will require an outflow of resources embodying economic benefits. The entity will be required to buy and retire carbon credits without receiving any resources embodying economic benefits in exchange.\\\\nReliable estimate\\\\nThe final criterion for recognising a provision is that a reliable estimate can be made of the amount of the obligation.\\\\nParagraph 25 of IAS 37 states that 'except in extremely rare cases, an entity will be able to determine a range of possible outcomes and can therefore make an estimate of the obligation that is sufficiently reliable to use in recognising a provision’.\\\\nThe Committee concluded that in the fact pattern described, it is likely that the entity would be able to make a reliable estimate of the amount of a constructive obligation that satisfies the other recognition criteria.\\\\nConclusion on whether a provision is recognised\\\\nThe Committee concluded that in the fact pattern described:\\\\na.whether the entity’s statement of its commitments to reduce and offset its greenhouse gas emissions creates a constructive obligation to fulfil those commitments will depend on the facts of the statement and the circumstances surrounding it.\\\\nb.if the statement creates a constructive obligation:\\\\ni.the entity does not recognise a provision when it makes the statement in 20X0. At that time, the constructive obligation is not a present obligation as a result of a past event.\\\\nii.the entity does not recognise a provision between 20X0 and 20X9 because it does not have a present obligation as a result of a past event until it has emitted the greenhouse gases it has committed to offset.\\\\niii.as the entity emits greenhouse gases in 20X9 and in subsequent years, it will incur a present obligation to offset these past emissions. If the entity has not yet settled that obligation and a reliable estimate can be made of the amount of the obligation, the entity recognises a provision.\\\\nIf a provision is recognised, is the corresponding amount recognised as an expense or as an asset when the provision is recognised?\\\\nThe Committee observed that if a provision is recognised, the corresponding amount is recognised as an expense, rather than as an asset, unless it gives rise to—or forms part of the cost of—an item that qualifies for recognition as an asset in accordance with an IFRS Accounting Standard.\\\\nOther accounting and disclosure implications\\\\nThe Committee observed that, irrespective of whether an entity’s commitment to reduce or offset its greenhouse gas emissions results in the recognition of a provision, the actions the entity plans to take to fulfil that commitment could affect the amounts at which it measures its other assets and liabilities and the information it discloses about them, as required by various IFRS Accounting Standards.\\\\nConclusion\\\\nThe Committee concluded that the principles and requirements in IFRS Accounting Standards provide an adequate basis for an entity to determine:\\\\na.whether an entity’s commitment to reduce or offset its greenhouse gas emissions creates a constructive obligation for the entity;\\\\nb.the circumstances in which the entity recognises a provision for the costs of fulfilling a constructive obligation to reduce or offset its greenhouse gas emissions; and\\\\nc.if a provision is recognised, whether the corresponding amount is recognised as an expense or as an asset when the provision is recognised.\\\\nConsequently, the Committee decided not to add a standard-setting project to the work plan.]\\\"\\n        }\\n      ]\\n    }\\n  ]\\n}\"}","expires":1778937539798}
